# Thread: Hi from hopeful and very sick
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-hopeful-and-very-sick.9061/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Daizy — 2013-12-13T00:17:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-hopeful-and-very-sick.9061/post-103258>

From being totally healthy, two prescribed medications 17 years ago,
cascaded in torturous devastating SEVERE symptoms/illness.

A brief summary:

1\. Prescribed fluoroquinolone antibiotic. Fell unwell, palpitations, insomnia
and many more symptoms.
2\. A few months later, prescribed Lariam - caused neurological dysfunction, including ocular toxicity, (inc visual distortion), floaters, halos, and more. Auditory
dysfunction such as hyperacusis, tinnitus, vestibular dysfunction, phototoxicity, severe reduced cognition, agnosia, type of aphasia, and more. Arrythmias, tachycardia daily for many years, up to 200 beats per minute, atrial flutters (not recently), AF, (not recently), bradycardia, very low BP, but can fluctuate to to high, overall too low.

Refused all meds to treat. I was hoping to improve with time, healthy diet and vits and supps. Weak to the point of being spoon fed, worsened to being bedridden/wheelchair bound for many years on/off, with lots of symptoms, including neuropathy.

A year later, unstable angina. Given IV GTN, which caused left ventricular dysfunction (heart failure),
Read a few years back, that IV GTN alters ALDH2 gene which causes LVF. Then, diastolic and systolic heart failure and global hypokinesis. Hypothyroidism, mitochondrial dysfunction, autonomic dysfunction, multiple food allergies (causing malnutrition and suspected scurvy), gallstones, toxic hepatitis. MCS, EMF, vascular (insufficiency, blood clots, right groin valve is dysfunctional, left foot turning blue etc..), and more symptoms.

Had thallium test - refused adenosine over ten years ago. Made me very ill. Angioscan with galdolinium, made me very ill, unstable angina for over two years, extreme headaches, hypo/hyperglycemic attacks, kidneys starting to fail etc.. and low grade on/off activation of CMV, EBV and HHV6 over a period of time, which I believe was activated by antibiotics, in particular amoxycillin, which I took over ten years ago. There is much evidence that antibiotics, in particular, amoxycillin can activate dormant infectious pathogens, in particular, the aforementioned.

There is a long list of symptoms, such as neuropathic pains, muscles pains, joints, bones, etc..

Since, I refused all invasive diagnostic tests, zero medication, no anesthetic (unable to tolerate, even local).Had several attacks of angina, unstable angina, acute coronary syndromes, suspected heart attacks for which I refused all medications. I take Arjuna, Ayurvedic med, no toxicity no reactions, from time to time.

Free radicals so high, that even blending a few vegetables makes me ill. Had oxygen mask once on, and made me iller for about a year. When pollution level is high, makes me very ill, angina, and more.

Many compounds in many foods cause worsening of weird neuro symptoms, and extreme pain in all veins and microvessels. Told some type of inflammatory vasculitis. Inability to metabolize some compounds in foods, makes me feel toxic, as if poisoned, and other symptoms. Found out some of the CYPs enzymes are dysfunctional including GSTS, which explains in part the inability to metabolize so many foods and meds.

Slight improvement since I started bone broths a year ago. Able to add a few more foods to the limited diet.
Unable to eat nuts, seeds, avocados, fish, dairy, I am very low in Omega 3 (as shown in tests), and most nurtrients and minerals.

Since the bone broths has been so helpful, I would like to continue with this. The only meats I can meat at present, are lamb and beef, including liver and kidney. I tried ox heart and could not tolerate. I would love to have been able to eat, it oozes with ubiquinol, as it boils..

Unable to eat all sea foods, except for kombu and dulse seaweeds, unable to tolerate others.

As for vegetables and fruits, very limited, but expanding since starting bone broths.

Tried functional medicine, acupuncture (worsened symptoms), Ayurvedic medicine, (some helpful),
pranayama breathing (made me dangerously ill, specially heart and autonomic), Qi Gong, (made me much
worse), Mudras (type of yoga using finger tips), had the opposite effect, homeopathy, (will try again), antroposophical medicine, (worsened symptoms). Met with several Qi Gong sifus for qi transmission. One caused severe symptoms lasting six months. The second sifu, was helpful, except for the last session suffered hypoxia, and oxygen sat went down to about 90. Similar experience with three other sifus and their prescribed exercises, which consisted of specific gestures, not moves/exercises.

It was explained that ion channels are so messed up, and intracellular communication so dysfunctional, that energy medicine worsens symptoms. Qi transmission practitioners say the Qi is too weak, and too much cell apoptosis. Ayurvedic practitioner says the agni is too dysfunctional. Advised to avoid, meditate for a long period of time, before next attempt.

Sitting in the sun makes me very ill. I read up on this and found out, that it can increase free radicals..this is how sensitive. Unable to tolerate solvents, chemicals, includes essential oils, zero tolerance to coffee, and alcohol. Heat or cold, worsens symptoms considerably. Baseline improves during Spring and Autumnal months.

I don't believe detoxing is safe for me, and would like to reduce free radicals using a nutritional protocol, such as the Paleo protocol here. Interests include chemical compounds in various foods, epigenetic effects, reducing free radicals, best foods to reduce various types of toxicities, and rebuild at cellular level. Looking forward to improve, and exchange info.

### caroline — 2013-12-13T01:13:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-hopeful-and-very-sick.9061/post-103266>

Okay .... now your post showed up! I answered you in your other post ..... pm me if you want.......

### caroline — 2013-12-13T01:04:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-hopeful-and-very-sick.9063/post-103263>

Hi Daizy and welcome. Have you listened to Dr. Tim Jackson's free community wide webinar? You can find it on the home page. He is doing a "Heal your Hormones" bootcamp for us right now and giving us an amazing amount of information.

Have you had a Genova GI test? saliva ASI? 23&me? have you been checked for Lyme?

Maybe go to Dr. Jackson's website and have a big read as well as here of course.

Dr. Tim is part of a MTHFR support group ..... he has done podcasts that you can listen too.

If you can swing his Bootcamp I think that would be huge for you.

What is your non native EMF exposure? at home? at work? This is a huge problem for most and probably even bigger for you.

Please listen to Dr. K's podcasts- great info and easy to understand! ..... and do you have his book? The Epi-Paleo RX

Please keep reading and asking questions ....... Have a look at some of the journals in the journal section - Martin in particular....

# Thread: Hello and a question about Huntington's disease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-question-about-huntingtons-disease.9064/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 38
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Richelle — 2013-12-13T04:16:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-question-about-huntingtons-disease.9064/post-103281>

Hello. I am 46 years old and I have a CAG mutation of 42. I am "pre-symptomatic" - a term I dont agree with. It doesnt make sense. I was born with this mutation and it is in every cell of my body. I have been studying health and human biology (top marks in biochemistry and molecular biology) and following your blog for a number of years. Thanks to things I have learned I have reversed my thyroid issues and prediabetes. I have removed all my amalgams and stabilized gum disease. I get out in the sun and walk barefoot in the dirt everyday.I have been following a mostly ketogenic diet for 10years. I have read Terry Wahls stuff and improved my nutrient consumption. I mange my stress with exercise and yoga (and retiring from work). I have good sleep habits and sleep very well. I take a range of supplements to support my mitochondria. I realize neurosurgeons dont normally deal with HD patients but my neurologist hasnt a clue and isnt interested in discussing anything with me. It is very difficult for me to find a doctor who will treat me as a whole person. And they get snotty if I ask questions they dont like. I do have a good GP but he is over run with Lyme disease patients and hard to get in to see. For the endocrine disturbance I am referred to one specialist and then for the psychiatric symptoms (depression/anxiety) I am referred to another etc. HD falls under the domain of Neurology but he is only interested in movement disorder and since I am not exhibiting any yet he just waves me away (after my annual 10 minutes with him) to come back in a year. My GP prescribed antioxidant therapy of 3mg melatonin, and also testosterone 4mg troche daily and progesterone 50mg to take from day 14 of my menstrual cycle (which is spot on 28 days). I spend a lot of money buying antioxidants and supplements aimed at supporting cellular and mitochondrial function. The mutant huntingtin protein impairs mitochondrial function in a number of different ways including electron transport, gene transcription, apoptosis and calcium handling. I read a lot of journal articles and, whilst my understanding has improved considerably over recent years, I am throwing anti-oxidants at my mitochondria in a rather hap hazard way and I was just wondering if Dr Kruse had any specific advice for me?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T05:04:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-question-about-huntingtons-disease.9064/post-103285>

Very very complex issue and one I have not tackled except in the current blog......Huntington's is tied to tertiary and quaternary protein folding due to transition metals. In my opinion 100% due to EMF non native......even the genetic component.

1.[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22393169](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22393169)
2.[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20399203](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20399203)
3.[http://www.pranabio.com/downloads/M... Prana comments on Arch of Neurol HD Data.pdf](http://www.pranabio.com/downloads/Media%20Releases/Media%20Releases%202012/MAy%201%202012%20Prana%20comments%20on%20Arch%20of%20Neurol%20HD%20Data.pdf)

### Richelle Jones — 2018-06-22T02:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-question-about-huntingtons-disease.9064/post-244010>

> [Richelle said:](/goto/post?id=103281)
>
> Hello. I am 46 years old and I have a CAG mutation of 42. I am "pre-symptomatic" - a term I dont agree with. It doesnt make sense. I was born with this mutation and it is in every cell of my body. I have been studying health and human biology (top marks in biochemistry and molecular biology) and following your blog for a number of years. Thanks to things I have learned I have reversed my thyroid issues and prediabetes. I have removed all my amalgams and stabilized gum disease. I get out in the sun and walk barefoot in the dirt everyday.I have been following a mostly ketogenic diet for 10years. I have read Terry Wahls stuff and improved my nutrient consumption. I mange my stress with exercise and yoga (and retiring from work). I have good sleep habits and sleep very well. I take a range of supplements to support my mitochondria. I realize neurosurgeons dont normally deal with HD patients but my neurologist hasnt a clue and isnt interested in discussing anything with me. It is very difficult for me to find a doctor who will treat me as a whole person. And they get snotty if I ask questions they dont like. I do have a good GP but he is over run with Lyme disease patients and hard to get in to see. For the endocrine disturbance I am referred to one specialist and then for the psychiatric symptoms (depression/anxiety) I am referred to another etc. HD falls under the domain of Neurology but he is only interested in movement disorder and since I am not exhibiting any yet he just waves me away (after my annual 10 minutes with him) to come back in a year. My GP prescribed antioxidant therapy of 3mg melatonin, and also testosterone 4mg troche daily and progesterone 50mg to take from day 14 of my menstrual cycle (which is spot on 28 days). I spend a lot of money buying antioxidants and supplements aimed at supporting cellular and mitochondrial function. The mutant huntingtin protein impairs mitochondrial function in a number of different ways including electron transport, gene transcription, apoptosis and calcium handling. I read a lot of journal articles and, whilst my understanding has improved considerably over recent years, I am throwing anti-oxidants at my mitochondria in a rather hap hazard way and I was just wondering if Dr Kruse had any specific advice for me?

Reading QT13 Methionine blog reminded me of my very first post here way back in 2013 when I said I was "throwing antioxidants at my mitochondria in a rather haphazard way" and I have read and learnt so much since then. Back then I was told that I was "hypomethylating" and so I was taking methionine, cysteine, creatine, tryptophan, lysine, (all of the amino acids) all the B vitamins separately, glutathione, NAC, SAMe, Magnesium, potassium, Calcium D glucarate, zinc piccolinate, ubiquinol, curcumin, resveratrol,quercitin, vitamins A,E,C,D, selenium, iodine, DHA, probiotics...thats all that i can remember...there are probably more and back then my homocysteine was high, LDL high, vitamin D under 40, BUN/creatine high and my sex hormones, melatonin, cortisol were all upside down...now i know why. Never again. I did that for about 5 years. I dont have ANY of those problems anymore.

### Richelle Jones — 2018-07-10T14:53:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-question-about-huntingtons-disease.9064/post-245513>

[https://www.nature.com/articles/srep43030](https://www.nature.com/articles/srep43030) so in this study that was done on transgenic sheep...CAG 75 and deemed to be presymptomatic they are presumably not affected by artificial light or other non native EMF effects....these sheep had significant defects in 80 of 130 measured metabolites including sphingolipids, biogenic amines, amino acids and urea. citrulline and arginine increased significantly.
things that increased = citrulline, arginine, NO pathway, urea, hydroxyproline, kynurenine, carnosine, alpha amino adipate pathway, asymmetric dimethylarginine,
things that decreased = sarcosine, valine, PPARGC1A, glutamine, threonine, sphingolipids, methionine, cytosine, isoleucine, leucine, tyrosine, alanine, phenylalanine,

and tryptophan, glutathione, cystathione gamalysase and glyercorphospho lipids were disrupted.

so what is it about the gene mutation that increases and decreases these metabolites and how could someone combat these?

### drezy — 2018-08-03T16:44:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-a-question-about-huntingtons-disease.9064/post-247391>

I hesitated to even share this thought, but what the hell.

Doing some reading on the blogs on Docs take on hemochromatosis, I started really rethinking snps and most genetic diseases (esp those that typically don't manifest until after a period of fertility).

I think if I'm following along correctly genetic disorders/diseases are generated/captured/implemented on a way faster than Darwinian timeframe in order to confer and advantage against a particular environmental or pathogenic challenge.

So what may seem like a faustian pact ma be more like nature iterating in order to find an ever-changing "correct" combination.

[https://jackkruse.com/cpc4-evolutionary-friend-or-foe/](https://jackkruse.com/cpc4-evolutionary-friend-or-foe/)

### drezy — 2018-08-03T17:05:59+0300

[https://biology.stackexchange.com/q...-variable-age-of-onset-in-huntingtons-disease](https://biology.stackexchange.com/questions/7967/what-factors-govern-the-variable-age-of-onset-in-huntingtons-disease)

Directly from a study in the link:
"**confirming that approximately 40% of the variance remaining in onset age is attributable to genes other than the HD gene and 60% is environmental"
**
No huge surprise to anyone here, right.

That's an incredible data point/individual

### Richelle Jones — 2018-08-04T01:08:50+0300

Haha yes I hope I am that outlier dot.... I do wonder why mother nature saved this gene...it must have some purpose. HD people have lower rates of some cancers. HD seems to have originated in white europeans and then spread to places where sailors visited. Tasmania (my ancestors) & Venezuela are particular hot spots...It is least common in Asians and Africans. mHTT has several haplotypes (did they emerge independently) and is associated with particular SNPs and disease symptoms vary with different ethnicities. Also the normal HTT gene has a variable CAG repeat and how they interact with the mHTT is not understood. Strangely people homozygous for mHTT do not develop disease earlier but do have more severe disease when they do. The role of the normal Huntingtin protein is varied and not fully understood but is essential from embryogenesis to adult and is ubiquitously expressed. The huge variability in age of onset and disease severity is obvious in my family. My grandmother was an identical twin and she and her sister had very different age of onset and disease symptoms. that was my first clue that environment matters waaaay before i knew anything about epigenetics. in fact that was the entire reason i got a degree in science so i could learn more about it.

Just looking in my family...my grandmother (inherited CAG41 from her mother and mito haplo U5) aged 28 passed the same CAG of 41 to my father... who was aged 21 and my mother (mito haplo H) aged 22 when I was born and I got a CAG 42....just 4 years later my brother got a CAG49. At age 30 I passed CAG 42 to my daughter. Sperm lose their mitochondria at conception so I wonder what mitonuclear coaptation effects there are for females passing HD on and whether mitochondrial haplotypes have different effects because when you inherit from your father you are getting the HD gene but it has never met the mothers mitochondria before...i wonder if that has any effect.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-rapid-epigenetic-expression-hsp-90.9065/post-103290>

This may answer some questions regarding the speed of evolutionary adaptations. Think of winding the DNA more tightly or exposing some parts of it more than others. All these alterations and more will change the expression of some genes relative to others. They are different forms of epigenetics than methylation or acetylation and quite responsive to aspects of the environment. **Heat shock proteins seem to be a way to alter protein folding and DNA shape and therefore expression.**

### Inger — 2013-12-15T12:03:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9066/post-103487>

I am too obsessed with negative entropy/structure/the word organized, it wont let my mind anymore!

I was making a vortex in my seaweed water with a wooden spoon and it made me think about how everything good have to be organized. Not only energy, but organized energy to be a good thing. Unorganized it could be very bad. Does not help one bit that there is lots of it. It is like when I put on my make up, it want be pretty if it is without symmetry and the color does not fit together in harmony..... Peter speaks about sublime... gait... those things. They all have to do with discipline and structure and negative entropy I realize.

I was thinking about religion too... and how it can help people to get into better ways of living, as they get more organized because of the rules and stuff. But there sadly is the thing that most get stuck? Like they forget that to the whole picture also belongs the never ending evolution..... negative entropy cannot not evolve me thinks, so if we always do the same, believe the same and behave the same it cannot be good? And that is when the religion that once was our blessing turns to our curse? too many thoughts in my mind lately

### prAna303 — 2013-12-16T13:24:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9066/post-103570>

I think and act in another way...

For me i always felt that the world around me where one day order and next chaos. These two states are like a coin, a single flip and everything changes, the good gets bad and the other way round. What is it that makes these switches? I have felt my whole life that i like to be in a mode where i navigate thru these polarities, can not have positive without the negative. I experience life with these sides of the coin, one is feeding and one is breaking me down.

My idea is that i as a unit will always choose how a thing affect me, if i get to much joy i must put myself in some sadness also. If i would not take chances i end up in a lukewarm death state, maximal entropy, information is chaotic. Order comes from how nature does it, acts with good and bad, warm and cold, night and day... On and on.

> I knew that people who claim to not be religious often seek order in other ways, often ending up treating that as a religion, also. Avoiding entropy.

So often this goes the wrong way, depending on how your personal state is, it goes to the extreme. I have many friends that are deep into meditation, yoga and stuff but the often seem to hit every little bump in their life. I feel that they focus everything on a vision that everything is perfect, everythings must be nice and warm. They run the other way when anything dark is coming. Same with the ones that goes and seek only bad things, oh my, why do everything happen to just ME? Have you seen that?

I feel that we are quite unique because we can make our own world and that also makes us quite messed up. The key is to be the captain and sail with precision, using every source of energy, good and bad but never get stuck.

### SeaHorse — 2013-12-16T14:00:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-2013-webinar.9066/post-103572>

I see the same thing with emotions....people get stuck at the top and bottom register...or numb out right in the middle...how much more music can you make if you use all the notes?
With Chinese element theory the same thing applies...you don't want to be all one element. Artists that have too much air without any earth suffer....similarly to people who are only earthy and never lets their creativity soar.

For me balance doesn't mean staying in the middle, it means being able to negotiate through chaos with agility and that leads to an engagement with life that is exhilarating. That might be the sense of perfection that people are grabbing onto...but it doesn't come from avoiding the shadows or the dark places....over focussing on order, like Inger said, often comes from fear

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-13T07:00:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-i-noticed-in-brain-gut-5.9067/post-103301>

So, in brain gut 5 I noticed this quote:
"The molecular magic is so impressive, that Roger Penrose has shown how consciousness can be explained by the quantum effects of this one fatty acid. In this molecular arrangement it would allow for gamma coherence of the neural microtubules found in the brainâ€™s substance".
Now, I'm pretty well acquainted with the theory of quantum consciousness and, as built by Roger Penrose and Stuart Hammeroff, it has, well, nothing to do with fatty acids, DHA or otherwise. Did Dr. Kruse make a mistake here or maybe he connected his theories with Penrose's without elaborating on the connection. I hope this wasn't distortion of scientific research done by Penrose, that would be a shame. Can anyone clarify with regards to this? Dr. Kruse?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T07:59:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-i-noticed-in-brain-gut-5.9067/post-103302>

I did not elaborate because not enough framework is laid........the Decemeber webinar and I expect the questions to be pointed in this direction........when your arrow is pointed to quantized biology your theory of everything has to point to molecular dynamic simulations. Penrose was looking that way with DHA because of the pi electron clouds. That is what makes DHA special.

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-13T17:03:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-i-noticed-in-brain-gut-5.9067/post-103327>

But Penrose's theories of consciousness only address microtubule quantum effects. I don't believe he's ever adressed the idea that DHA may play a role. Are you suggesting his theories are incomplete because he doesn't include the quantum biological effects of DHA?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T23:43:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-i-noticed-in-brain-gut-5.9067/post-103355>

No....Penrose and Hameroff are looking at one aspect of neurologic function. I am looking at how the entire brain works in all systems. The pi electron cloud is part of that control of the microtubule effects.

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T15:28:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientist-finding-out-just-how-good-cold-can-be-now-daily.9071/post-103318>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/12/131206101611.htm](http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/12/131206101611.htm)

"Scientists covered the mice with blue latex to prevent evaporation from the skin and thus stop the heat loss. With this loss the mice stopped accumulating fat in the liver."

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T15:29:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientist-finding-out-just-how-good-cold-can-be-now-daily.9071/post-103319>

This is why in my CT protocol I tell you to wear a compression shirt during the protocol. It sensitizes you to cold at the surface level. But you must drink water prior to the cold exposure for many reasons. The biggest one is to make sure water is available around the compressed SQ collagen to allow for water electronic induction. This increases the electrons and protons available for action by cold using the Hall effect. All of this is laid out in detail in my latest series called energy and epigenetics and the the last blog talks about how the charge separation happens in water around collagen to drive the sympathetic nervous system to drive adipocytes to empty their contents for beta oxidation under the control of nitric oxide and increase blood flow.

When you know better you do better...........BOOM.

### Hemming — 2013-12-13T15:44:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientist-finding-out-just-how-good-cold-can-be-now-daily.9071/post-103321>

I was a little confused when they said that cooling of the skin led to breaking down of fat in their adipose tissue which was sent to the liver. I read it as they accumulated it in the liver and thereby developing fatty liver and visceral fat. But if I understand you correctly, you say the fat goes there to used for energy.

'...these mice had difficulties after weaning and accumulated fat in the liver. So this showed that the lack of the fat-binding protein in the skin was sufficient to induce accumulation of fat in the liver.'

I guess its more about if the water loss or heat loss was the cause of the accumulation of fat in the liver.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T16:28:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientist-finding-out-just-how-good-cold-can-be-now-daily.9071/post-103322>

Goes there for beta oxidation.......if you cant fat burn you got problems. This is why being LS is big.......it allows you to burn fat in your mitochondria by reversing electron chain transport flow thru cytochrome 2. When you eat carbs you are driving metabolism thru cytochrome 1 and raising NADH. This has major effects on the charge in your body. This charge is what the electromagnetic force acts upon.

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-13T16:34:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-near-a-military-base.9072/post-103323>

There's a house that's come up on the market which in many ways is pretty much perfect... the only problem is that it's about 3km from a base that's responsible for a nuclear submarine. I'm just not sure if this is too close but it's within budget and has all these features...

Large garden (third of an acre)
Elevated position (unlikely to get damp)
No street lights
Away from masts, pylons etc
Short walk from the beach (ie seawater CT)
Upstairs balcony
Walk-in condition
Boat mooring

Any thoughts?

I don't know if regular EMF meters will be good enough for detecting problems with living near a base like this.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-13T16:48:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-near-a-military-base.9072/post-103325>

No, I'm in the UK (look under my user name and you'll see I set my location as UK). So there must be several thousand miles between me and those dead dolphins on the east coast of America.

Another thing that can upset dolphins is the equipment they use for oil exploration...

"Oil and gas exploration and development includes intense noise caused by very loud seismic exploration, the placement of rigs and associated pollution and drilling, and finally, in decades to come, the removal of the rigs when the supply is exhausted. Should one licence be allowed, others will surely follow. This means increasing and ongoing disturbance for the dolphins. We do not want this precedent to be set."

[http://www.independent.co.uk/enviro...olphins-at-risk-from-oil-drilling-780700.html](http://www.independent.co.uk/environment/nature/scottish-dolphins-at-risk-from-oil-drilling-780700.html)

"During seismic surveys used in the exploration for oil and gas, high intensity, low frequency sounds (from air guns towed behind boats) are directed through the seabed and are reflected at the boundaries defining different geological layers. The reflected sound is then processed to reveal information about the rock strata and to identify potential hydrocarbon reservoirs. Shots are fired from the air guns at short intervals (usually between 6 and 20 seconds) along pre-determined routes (referred to as transect lines). Each transect line can be several hours long, surveys often last several months and consist of hundreds of transects. These activities therefore greatly increase the underwater noise in the area in which they take place. A considerable amount of energy radiates from the air guns and noise from these surveys can travel for thousands of kilometres in the ocean, masking calls of whales that have historically communicated over considerable distances."

[http://us.whales.org/node/8238](http://us.whales.org/node/8238)

### prAna303 — 2013-12-15T19:47:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-near-a-military-base.9072/post-103531>

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-03T07:17:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/throwing-antioxidants-at-mitochondria-is-a-bad-move-real-bad.9073/post-108055>

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-03T07:30:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/throwing-antioxidants-at-mitochondria-is-a-bad-move-real-bad.9073/post-108056>

The free radical theory of aging that Harman proposed long ago that oxidized macromolecules accumulate with age to decrease cell function and shorten life-span has little support these days (Harman, 1968). Unfortunately today this is biologic dogma because no one else has any better ideas how mitochondria are really designed to work in nature. Recent experimental work has shown it is dead wrong. When your experiments do not match your theory, your theory is wrong. Moreover, recent nutritional and genetic interventions to boost antioxidants have generally failed to increase life-span. The overall result of 19 clinical trials finds supplementation with the lipid-soluble antioxidant vitamin E failed to reduce mortality (Miller et al., 2005). The water soluble antioxidant vitamin C is also generally ineffective in reducing all-cause mortality (Bjelakovic et al., 2007). An even more important test of the free radical theory of aging involves genetic overexpression of antioxidant enzymes. To date, increases in SOD, or catalase or a combination, while lowering oxidized macromolecules, fail to increase lifespan in mice (Perez et al., 2009). Only overexpression of the peroxide and redox active thioredoxin 1 (Mitsui et al., 2002) and mitochondrial targeted catalase (Schriner et al., 2005) have been shown to increase mouse lifespan. In addition, the free radical theory fails to explain why higher levels of oxy-radical damage occurs constantly with exercise (Powers et al., 2008), which generally promotes healthy human aging (Nakamura et al., 1996) and extends lifespan in some rodents (Navarro et al., 2004) (Holloszy et al., 1985). The answer is not increasing the ORAC levels of food. It is found in metabolic epigenetic redox switches that happen upstream to mitochondria to alter where and how subatomic particles are fed into the ETC when nutrients are delivered to this organelle for processing. . The metabolically initiated redox shift happens "upstream" of the commonly observed increase in reactive oxidative damage to lipid membranes, receptors, and macromolecules in tissues. This shift has been constantly observed experimentally in normal aging; It occurs in the oxidized direction of the relative levels of important reductants and oxidants in cells. It manifests itself as an extracellular decrease in the ratio of cysteine/cystine in tissues with a simultaneous intracellular decrease in the ratio of GSH/GSSG and NAD(P)H/NAD(P). The oxidized redox shift is initiated by low demand for bursts of energy produced by mitochondria. The loss of energy efficiency is the key signal This low demand for energy also is manifest by observed low levels of physical and mental activity in people with this issue. This initiates a vicious cycle of oxidized membrane receptors, signaling molecules, transcription factors and epigenetic transcriptional regulators on a chronic basis. This increase ubiquination and the process steepens with respect to time. Organ failure and aging increase with respect to chronologic age. Epigenetic factors modulated by aging include the histone deacetylase family which includes the sirtuins as well as histone acetylases and DNA methyltransferases. Together and collectively, the epigenetic mediators impose the metabolic shift away from use of mitochondrial energy toward reliance on glycolysis. All of these complex epigenetic coupled factors are tied to inflammation generation via the NRF2 and KEAP1 transcription factors. This is why inflammation appears to be tied to all these things we talk about in the blogosphere. The key is understanding what happens proximal to the mitochondria and then how the mitochondria respond.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-03T07:31:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/throwing-antioxidants-at-mitochondria-is-a-bad-move-real-bad.9073/post-108057>

Confusion occurs when you only focus on input, thru put or output. That is where PhD's and clinicians get lost because they do not have a full account of the 3 D atomic anatomy of the problem because they never even consider the atomic level. They are all on the macroscopic level. What happens inside them and upstream of them is far more important. Understanding aging is the best way to prevent illness. This metabolic shift is further mediated by insulin resistance since a sedentary life does not require the metabolic demands that need to be regulated by insulin. Collectively, this epigenetic oxidative redox shift in aging results in a downward spiral of inability to respond to energy demands or environmental stressors which leads to stress-induced catastrophic initiation of cellular death pathways and organ failure. The environment is what mitochondria respond to. It then sends that information to the nucleus making mitonuclear co-aptation a sign of the times and what epigenetics is telling us. When NAD+ drops we know redox inside a cell is decreased. Complex.......but understandable when you get what mitochondria are really designed to do with charges subatomic particles from our environment.

### Arthur Morris — 2013-12-13T20:57:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when.9075/post-103340>

I am taking a 40 minute Ice Bath in 58 degree water. I place three plastic bottles of ice on my chest when I like down up to my neck after 20 minutes. Without ice baths I get up every hour for a pit stop. With the ice baths I sleep 5-6 hours right off the bat and then sleep another hour or two after one pit stop.

I get to bed early and wake early. I am now eating 30 minutes after arising, eating lunch at 11am and eating dinner at 4:15pm so that I can not eat anything for five hours so that I can to sleep at 9:30 or so.

I used to take my ice bath at 5pm and have dinner at 6pm.

With my new dinner at 4:15 pm schedule should I be taking my ice bath before or after dinner? If the latter, how long should I wait after dinner?

### caroline — 2013-12-13T21:09:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when.9075/post-103341>

# Thread: So Just What Is "Nutrition"???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-just-what-is-nutrition.9079/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2013-12-14T01:42:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-just-what-is-nutrition.9079/post-103366>

Considering where we are heading with the December Webinar and EE10, it seems to me that working on a new and unified theory of Biology begs a new and unified theory of "Nutrition". I spend part of my time in the wacky world of healthcare practice and the other part in the wacky world of health/superfoods. In both, people know me as the sobering voice of "reality". This often means that they would rather I do not speak. Currently I am reviewing the nutritional research on a popularly consumed commodity. All of the research focuses on quantities of nutrients in the commodity and a few studies on possible physiologic correlations with these nutrients and their known or suspected actions. None of them studies the entropy of a given sample or product. "Raw" is often considered more nutritious in both vegetarian and paleo worlds. The arguments are often around more nutrients in unprocessed foods and or higher quality or more "resonant" nutrients. To me this all translates to the relative entropy in the food. My initial thoughts include:

1\. When a food requires "processing" for consumption and assimilation whether heating, fermentation, etc.; does it optimize the "entropy"?
2\. If so, how would we measure this. For example, some people use Fritz Albert Popp's technology to measure the photonic potency and character of foods and herbs to demonstrate this. Some even make a case for characterizing organ systems this way and matching nutrients to them.
3\. Is their a way to quantify nutrient activity versus nutrient quantity in a given food? One approach is to measure assimilation through measuring levels intracellularly or in serum, and I wonder what resonant means might work such as infrared crystal spectroscopy that is used to characterize materials.
4\. How might we engineer foods that are far enough down the marine food chain to be sustainable and portable, but far enough up to have processed the nutrients into the optimal form? Is there anything below molluscs that fills this? For example, DHA is marketed derived from algae that purportedly feed the fish to accumulate the DHA. I have not confirmed this point, so it is anecdotal without a reference.
5\. I recently received a crowd sourcing solicitation for what looks like a handheld Raman Spectroscopy food scanner to work with mobile devices, so this is obviously on people's minds.

[http://tellspec.com](http://tellspec.com)

Strangely enough, a friend just asked me to recommend science projects for an educational institution that specializes in development issues including agriculture and relief efforts. I have worked on these in the past and last night I was reflecting on the complete disregard for established diets and the genetic matching of efforts when it comes to nutrition. America's attitude is typically "if SAD is good enough for us, it is good enough for you". All of us subsidize US farmers and part of these subsidies purchase surplus crops for "relief and development".

I in no way want to ignore the EPi-Paleo thread, I am looking for methodology to evaluate the relative "nutrition" value of food beyond the current laboratory methodology.

# Thread: Mitochondrial Leakiness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-leakiness.9081/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-14T03:49:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-leakiness.9081/post-103384>

I'm trying to better understand mitochondrial function, however each time I read something it seems to only further confuse the issue. If I understand Dr. Kruse's idea properly, he essentially suggests that glucose produces massive ROS (like superoxide) production in the mitochondria and that ketone/fat metabolism is preferable because it produces less ROS in the Electron Transport Chain. There are 3 major points of confusion I have.
1\. Dr. Kruse seems to suggests, in his leaky life post, benefits stemming from ROS production, such as reduced mutations and things. If this is true, then by his own theories glucose should provide see beneficial effects, right?
2\. Ketones and fats (which drive electrons through complex II) actually seem to produce far MORE ROS (in the form of superoxide) than glucose oxidation (which drives electrons through complex I).

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2012/07/protons-superoxide.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2012/07/protons-superoxide.html)
"Adding in glutamate mixed with malate is a classic combination for driving NADH utilisation through complex I. The mitochondria generate about 30pmol/min of H2O2 under these conditions. This is not a lot and some labs report the amount as being close to zero.
Driving complex II (succinate dehyrdogenase) directly, using succinate but not supplying any NADH generators, produces rather more H2O2, around 400pmol/min. A ten fold increase.
Clearly, this interferes with Dr. Kruse's idea that ketones/fat oxidation produce less ROS than glucose metabolism, right?

3\. If I'm not mistaken, Dr. Kruse believes that by decreasing the "Leakiness" of our mitochondria, we can be healthier. However, Leakiness of mitochondria (such as increase superoxide production) is what causes mitochondrial biogenesis, something we should certainly be after. So it seems like we should increase leaky mitochondria in order to create more superoxide to drive mitochondrial biogenesis.

### nonchalant — 2014-05-27T04:50:54+0300

> Go read Nick Lane's book Power Sex and Suicide for a primer on the context of how the mitochondria work. They are complex and counterintuitive because they are the ultimate quantum tunneler.

I've been reading this book. Good thing Jack has already introduced us to some of the concepts, makes it easier.

One awesome quote from p.11:

> "They're usually of bacterial size...Metabolically active cells...have hundreds or thousands of mitochondria, making up some 40% of the cytoplasm. The egg cell, or oocyte, is exceptional: it passes on around 100,000 mitochondria to the next generation." "All in all, there are said to be 10 million billion mitochondria in an adult human, which together constitute about 10% of our body weight."

10% of our weight in bacterial-sized units. We're crawling with mitochondria!

### nonchalant — 2014-05-27T04:58:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-leakiness.9081/post-128485>

And on p.15,

> Various antibodies work by blocking protein assembly in bacteria, and also block protein synthesis in the mitochondria, but not from the nuclear genes in the eukaryotes.

Not only to antibiotics wipe out our gut microbes, they also adversely affect our mitochondria? Mitochondria don't do a lot of protein synthesis, the cell nucleus does most of the work for them. Mito do synthesize the most critical proteins for cellular respiration, though, like the proteins that start building the various complexes, like ATPase, on the inner membrane. Holy protein-folding, Batman!

It is messy but in the text files called succinic acid will be summaries of it. I don't think it has the diabates studies in it just google "succinate diabetes NCBI" for them, and copy paste DOI numbers into sci hub for full articles. Here is the start of one of the summaries on succinic acid:

In Western countries, the coenzyme Q10 is very popular. In some experiments, coenzyme Q10 showed the ability to express prolonged life. But here that is interesting; There is a drug that plays in the body a role similar to coenzyme Q10. It is also, and perhaps more effective, at a price that is about 10 times cheaper. It's succinic acid. Perhaps, it is because of such a low price that amber acid is not promoted to the market.
We should pay special attention to succinic acid (hereinafter YaK)! By the ratio of price / effectiveness, this thing is almost the leader among the means to prolong life. Yak is a natural substance (present in all organisms), has a powerful healing effect, without causing side effects and addictive. There are many reasons for this, but there are basic reasons.
Amber acid:

1\. Stimulates the production of energy in cells. With age, in addition to other disorders, the cells of the body begin to lose the ability to generate energy. As a consequence, the body begins to lack energy for the normal provision of many of its functions, and this leads to the aging of the body and its rapid aging. The main substance in the body that gives energy is ATP (adenosine triphosphate). So, succinic acid helps to produce ATP. As a consequence, it is a powerful stimulator of energy production and a stimulator of many functions of the body, has an exceptional restorative power.
2\. Enhances cellular respiration, promotes the assimilation of oxygen by cells, tissues, organs. For example, the increase in the rate of oxygen consumption by liver cells with the addition of YaK increases 60-fold.
3\. Disinfects free radicals. Has a powerful antioxidant property. As it was said more than once, in the process of vital activity of the body aggressive forms of oxygen are formed which oxidize or destroy cells, causing diseases (cancer, heart attack, etc.), aging and death. Yak can effectively neutralize free radicals.
In general, the effect of YaK on the body is enormous. She:

Inside the mitochondria, succinate is one of the most prominent intermediates of the Krebs
cycle. Succinate oxidation in mitochondria provides the most powerful energy output per
unit time. Extra-mitochondrial succinate triggers a host of succinate receptor (SUCN1 or
GPR91)-mediated signaling pathways in many peripheral tissues including the hypothalamus. One of the actions of succinate is to stabilize the hypoxia and cellular stress conditions by inducing the transcriptional regulator HIF-1a. Through these actions, it is hypothesized
that succinate has the potential to restore the gradual but significant loss in functions
associated with cellular senescence and systemic aging​

Succinate is an essential component of the Krebs or citric acid cycle and serves an electron donor in the production of fumaric acid and FADH2. It also has been shown to be a good "natural" antibiotic because of its relative acidic or caustic nature (high concentrations can even cause burns). Succinate supplements have been shown to help reduce the effects of hangovers by activating the degradation of acetaldehyde - a toxic byproduct of alcohol metabolism - into CO2 and H2O through aerobic metabolism. Succinic acid has been shown to stimulate neural system recovery and bolster the immune system. Claims have also been made that it boosts awareness, concentration and reflexes.

### Josh — 2015-01-17T23:09:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-are-what-you-eat-but-the-light-it-occurs-in-matters-most.9093/post-153189>

Somehow this never got added to this....

> -   This team of researchers at Nanjing University had been studying the miRNAs that circulate in human blood and were surprised to find that some of the miRNAs weren’t homegrown but instead came from plants. One of the most common plant miRNAs was from rice, a staple of their Chinese subjects’ diets. Intrigued, they confirmed with a variety of tests in mice that the miRNA, which, in its native environs, usually regulates plant development, was definitely coming from food.
> -   When they put the rice miRNA in cells, they found that levels of a receptor that filters out LDL, aka [“bad” cholesterol](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LDL), in the liver went down. As it turned out, the miRNA was binding to the receptor’s messenger RNA and preventing it from being expressed, sending receptor levels down and bad-cholesterol levels up. They saw the same effect when they tried it in mice.
> -   Going further, when they fed rice to mice but also gave them a molecule that would turn off the miRNA, the liver receptor bounced back and bad cholesterol levels went down.
> -   The team concludes that miRNAs may be a new class of functional components in food, like vitamins or minerals—even in an animal that’s pretty far removed from their home organism, they can manipulate gene expression and have an effect on nutrition.

[http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/8...stion-and-alter-gene-expression/#.VLrPbCvF98F](http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/80beats/2011/09/21/what-you-eat-affects-your-genes-rna-from-rice-can-survive-digestion-and-alter-gene-expression/#.VLrPbCvF98F)

[http://www.nature.com/cr/journal/v22/n1/full/cr2011158a.html](http://www.nature.com/cr/journal/v22/n1/full/cr2011158a.html)

### Josh — 2015-01-17T23:17:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-are-what-you-eat-but-the-light-it-occurs-in-matters-most.9093/post-153190>

> miRNAs from plants can enter animal cells and regulate their metabolic processes, a pair of botanists in France report. Micro RNAs (miRNAs) are short strands (typically 20 to 25 nucleotides) of non-protein coding RNA that bind to complementary sequences on mRNAs (much longer, protein coding RNAs) and modify their expression, usually by silencing them. They affect most animal and plant genes, although somewhat differently in plants.
> “The authors of this study hypothesized that epithelial cells in the intestine might take up miRNAs in food, package them into MVs \[plasma microvesicles\] and release them into the circulatory sytem. The secreted MVs could then deliver exogenous plant miRNA to target organs where they could regulate cognate mRNAs.”
>
> This expectation was upheld. Thus we see a way for an animal species, even humans, to acquire small genetic subroutines from the plant world. In this study the acquired miRNAs were observed to affect only somatic cells, and not germline cells. Still, very interesting.
>
> Hervé Vaucheret and Yves Chupeau, “Ingested plant miRNAs regulate gene expression in animals”
>
> HTML:
>
> ```
> , doi:10.1038/cr.2011.164, p3-5 v22, Cell Research, Jan 2012.
> Thanks, Monica C. Vella and Frank J. Slack, for the illustration of the “Dicer” miRNA in C. elegans from their online WormBook.
> Thanks for the alert, Jeff Krolick.
> Viruses and Other Gene Transfer Mechanisms is the main related local webpage. What’sNEW about HGT |
>
> For all the vegans who think grasses and plants are so safe……..they
> [/QUOTE]
>
> http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-2-viral-marketing/
>
> [QUOTE]
> [SIZE=4][B]Abstract[/B][/SIZE]
> Grapes produce large amounts of polyphenols. Many of them accumulate in the skin, pulp, and seeds and are consequently found in wine. The health benefits of a moderate consumption of wine have been attributed at least in part to grape's polyphenols. Among them, resveratrol (3,5,4'-trihydroxystilbene) is a phytoalexin that stimulates plant cell defenses against infections and also plays protective roles in humans, where it delays cardiovascular alterations and exerts anticancer and anti-inflammatory effects. Despite numerous studies, the molecular mechanisms of resveratrol action are only partially understood. Given its pleiotropic effects, it was previously suggested that resveratrol protective properties may arise from its modulation of the expression of microRNAs. Therefore, this review will focus on the effects of resveratrol on microRNA populations in humans and human cell lines, especially emphasizing the microRNAs that have been implicated in resveratrol effects on inflammation, cancer, metabolism, and muscle differentiation.
>
> [/QUOTE]
>
> http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22571175
>
> [QUOTE]he first problem that most of the bone experts, including Darwin had, was to explain how humans are the only mammal to become bipedal. For 150 million years Mother Nature constructed an amazing mammalian body plan for the quadraped spine. To date, no theory yet, has solved the human riddle of bipedalism. I think the answer lies in retrotransposons of assimilated viruses and from miRNA from foods to drive genetic changes that later manifested in homo’s body plans changes we see today. It also occurred because the rapid changes had to be first passed from the environment to our innate immune systems for processing and modifications.  Our guts are the only way nutrients get in and out of our body.  Our immune system sits right behind the brush border so the morphology follows the design where “[B]form meets a function[/B].”  There is no way we could go from the trees eating fruits and fibrous roots/bark  to a meat and fat eating human without some alterations. This implied to me that once the gut became leaky it began to shorten because of environmental triggers in the diet. It also implied the environmental antigens we faced [B]upon the ground [/B]occurred in a new [B]COLD and WET[/B] environment of the Pliocene. These environments were new challenges for the transitional primates to face. This meant their immune systems would also have to adapt to the rapid climate changes because it brought with it new pathogens to protect itself from.[/QUOTE]
>
> http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-3-look-in-the-past-to-see-your-prologue/

# Thread: Plants won't grow near Wi-Fi routers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plants-wont-grow-near-wi-fi-routers.9095/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-12-15T15:03:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plants-wont-grow-near-wi-fi-routers.9095/post-103495>

> The experiment began when the five students realized that they had difficulty concentrating in school if they slept near their mobile phones the previous night. Intrigued by this phenomenon, the students endeavored to study the effects of cellphone radiation on humans. Unfortunately, their school prevented them from pursuing this experiment due to a lack of resources, so the students decided to test the effects of Wi-Fi radiation (comparable in strength to cellphone radiation) on a plant instead.
>
> The girls placed six trays of Lepidium sativum seeds (a garden cress grown commercially throughout Europe) in a room without radiation, and an equal amount in a room next to two Wi-Fi routers. Over a 12-day period, they observed, measured, weighed and photographed the results. Even before the 12th day arrived, however, the end results were obvious: The cress seeds placed near the routers either hadn't grown or were completely dead, while the seeds placed in the radiation-free room had blossomed into healthy plants.
>
> The experiment earned the five students top honors in a regional science competition. Moreover, according to a teacher at their school, Kim Horsevad, a professor of neuroscience at the Karolinska Institute in Sweden was so impressed with the experiment that he is interested in repeating it in a controlled scientific environment.

[http://www.naturalnews.com/043238\_Wi-Fi\_routers\_radiation\_plant\_growth.html#](http://www.naturalnews.com/043238_Wi-Fi_routers_radiation_plant_growth.html#)

The article also referenced this:

> **34 Scientific Studies Showing Adverse Biological Effects + Damage From Wi-Fi**
>
> Here is a collection of scientific papers finding adverse biological effects or damage to health from Wi-Fi signals, Wi-Fi-enabled devices or Wi-Fi frequencies (2.4 or 5 GHz), complied by campaign group WiFi In Schools. The papers listed are only those where exposures were 16V/m or below. Someone using a Wi-Fi-enabled tablet computer can be exposed to electromagnetic fields up to 16V/m. Papers are in alphabetical order.

[http://stopsmartmeters.org.uk/papers-finding-adverse-biological-effects-damage-to-health-from-wi-fi/](http://stopsmartmeters.org.uk/papers-finding-adverse-biological-effects-damage-to-health-from-wi-fi/)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-15T17:48:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plants-wont-grow-near-wi-fi-routers.9095/post-103507>

[http://vimeo.com/81534630](http://vimeo.com/81534630)

### Hope — 2013-12-23T01:09:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plants-wont-grow-near-wi-fi-routers.9095/post-104089>

I so badly want to live on a desert island. Or on the top of a mountain with no one closer than 4 miles.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ivig-patient.9101/post-103511>

Hello,

I am a 60 yr old man with an auto immune disease that requires IV gamma globulin every 3 or 4 weeks. Its a multifocal blockage of conduction disease. I also have a thyroid disorder that requires a prescrip for synthyroid. The neuros are doing their best to stabilize my condition. Its been about 10 years since I started getting IVIG.

Since reading Dr Kruze's writings and following his advice I am beginning to feel less bothered by inflammation. I especially notice that I get less exercise induced inflammation after workouts. I am also finding my optimal weight easier to attain.

There are so many testimonials here that its encouraging.

Be well, wishing everyone optimal health in the upcoming year,

### nonchalant — 2013-12-16T03:38:49+0200

>
> Do you ever worry about paralytic shellfish poisoning?

We had some difficulty getting raw oysters a few months ago, evidently there were problems with the oysters not passing inspection. Finally they got some oysters in and I ate some. I notice a slight tingle to my lips and the tip of my tongue as I was finishing the plate full. I had no problems, and the tingle soon went away, but it was a month before I tried oysters again. Thankfully, the next batch had no such issues for me.

# Thread: Are You A Psychopath?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-you-a-psychopath.9103/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2013-12-15T19:11:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-you-a-psychopath.9103/post-103527>

I watched an incredibly interesting documentary last night about psychopaths, who they really are and how to tell if someone is one or not. There's an online quiz to see where you are on the "spectrum", my score was very low. It seems we actually need a few psychopaths around because they are the ones who are willing to takes risks, although it can backfire and result in the financial mess we're seeing in recent years.

[http://psychopath.channel4.com/quizzes.html](http://psychopath.channel4.com/quizzes.html)

> When most people think of psychopaths, Hannibal Lecter typically springs to mind! But actually, being psychopathic doesn't necessarily mean that you're a serial killer â€“ or even that you'll break the law.
>
> Some of us may score higher on some psychopathic traits than on others. But unless you score high on all of them, you don't really have anything to worry about! Another misconception about psychopaths concerns diagnosis. A lot of people think that you're either a psychopath or you're not, that it's all very black and white. But there is evidence to suggest that it's not as clear-cut as this. In fact, psychopathy â€“ like height and weight, for example â€“ lies on a spectrum.
>
> This test is not a diagnostic tool nor is it psychometrically validated. It cannot be used to clinically diagnose psychopathic personality or any mental illness.
>
> Within the framework of clinical psychology, a psychopath is someone with a distinct cluster of personality traits including ruthlessness, fearlessness, narcissism, charm, charisma, impulsivity, persuasiveness, manipulation and a lack of conscience and empathy. Sure, these traits may well come in handy if you aspire to be an axe-murderer! But they can also come in handy in the courtroom, on the trading floor, or in the operating theatre. It just depends on what else you've got going on in your personality, and the start you get in life.
>
> At the sharp end you may well find your serial killers and axe murderers but, all of us have our place at some point along the continuum.

This is the one question I have been thinking about - especially since I took the test pretending to be Dr. K.

At first I thought ...No...and then I thought ...... maybe.

In retrospect - I am thinking HELL YES. Not in a mean way - or a sneaky way ..... but after working with so many people over the years - Lots of folks are lazy and very unmotivated and very unfocused and want everything to be handed to them. I am definitely NOT going to stand in line behind them .... no way, no how.

this is my journey to do with what I can and what I am able ....to the best of my abilities. At the final curtain call I need to be able to say - I did my best and I gave it everything I had and hopefully had a blast!

# Thread: Rerun - here I am... and now what?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 347
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Andrea — 2013-12-16T17:33:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-103590>

This fall I found this intriguing site and I have read a lot of articles, but there still are many, many more to read...

My background:
Female, 37 years old.
Started noticing hypothyroid symptoms in 2007, but wasn’t diagnosed until 2011. I got prescription for Levothyroxine (synthetic T4), which made me feel better, but I still lacked energy - until I got a prescription for Liothyronine (T3) half a year later.
In 2012 I was on 75 mcg T4 + 20 mcg T4 (split on 3 times a day) and I was doing somewhat ok, but I had this notion that something was wrong. For reasons I can’t explain I stopped eating the T4 (supplementing only with T3). Two weeks later I felt much better than I had done for several years, but another week or two later I started to get my old hypothyroid symptoms back. So I started to take a low dose of T4, about 31 mcg/day. I got much, much better. In January 2013 I started taking CoQ10, which I believe has made me more stable and less sensitive to the exact timing of my T3 med.
This summer I was bothered by my high resting heart rate of around 75 and "extra beats" every now and then, so I decreased the T3 to totally 15 mcg/day (3x 5 mcg). My resting heart rate is now 60-65 and I feel pretty ok.

My latest lab tests were taken last week and I got the results today.
T4: 49 nmol/L (ref. 66-181)
T4 free: 7.7 pmol/L (ref. 12-22)
T3: 0.66 nmol/L (ref. 1.3-3.1)
TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (ref. 0.30-4.2)
It doesn't look too good, does it?

Unfortunately, they don’t test for rT3 in my country. They are restrictive with testing in general, so there’s no way I can make my doc do any tests of my choice.

As for my diet, I’ve been on a low carb high fat diet since 2010. This has helped me to somewhat maintain the same weight over these years (still 10 kg (20 lb) more than before I started to gain weight in 2007 though). However, after going low carb my LDL went up quite drastically. From my "normal" 3.5-4 mmol/L to 15 mmol/L. It hasn’t decreased much since. A couple heart specialists have tried to feed me with statins, but so far I have managed to refuse...

Last month I was on a 10 day trip at a nice warm place and chose to eat everything that seemed tasty - for me the local cuisine is a part of the experience when travelling - and I started Leptin Rx when I got home.

(To be continued...)

_EDIT: 15 mmol/L LDL = 580 mg/dl, for those who are more familiar with these units._

### Andrea — 2013-12-16T17:37:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-103591>

Iâ€™ve never been a breakfast person, so I find it real hard to eat in the morning, especially proteins. For the last year or two Iâ€™ve mostly eaten only once a day, in the afternoon.

For the last month Iâ€™ve eaten about 150 g prawns with a couple tablespoons mayonnaise (homemade, from macadamia+olive oil) and 2 fried eggs for breakfast. The next meal is normally dinner when I get home from work.
Dr.K. has written that one can replace some of the protein with fat, but I havenâ€™t been able to find how this replacement should be done - by weight, volume, calories, or...? But I do eat as much as I can... However, I really would like to go back to only eating dinner.
Since I started with these breakfasts, I have become a bit bigger; most noticeably in my stomach area and I look as if I was pregnant. Not so nice, really.

Iâ€™m not really sure how to handle my medication and breakfast. My doc tells me to take my T4 and T3 on an empty stomach and preferably not eat for about an hour afterwards - which wouldnâ€™t work with dr.K.â€™s instruction to eat breakfast within 30 minutes from getting up. For now I stay in bed and try to rest until 5:30 am, then take my medication and have breakfast just before 6 am.

One problem is that my sleep has got really crappy lately. I think it has to do with me wearing blue blocker glasses in the evening. With the glasses Iâ€™m not just tired in the evening, I actually feel sleepy. Yet, I find it difficult to fall asleep. I go to bed at around 9 pm, and wake up around midnight - 1 am. Then I roll around sleepless more or less until itâ€™s time to get up.
I have to say I slept much better before I started to wear the blue blocker glasses!

As for EMF thereâ€™s not much I can do at this point: I live in an apartment in a city. I am looking for a house out of town, although it may take time before I find what I want. But at least I have moved my phone and clock away from my bed to the other side of the room.

Anyway, my mood has become more stable lately and I feel generally more laid back and positive, which is great.

I thought I would start lifting weights (HIT Ã  la "Body by Science") now, but considering my low T3 Iâ€™m not so sure. I suppose I should increase my T3 dose again before I start training. Possibly decrease T4 so that my heart doesnâ€™t speed up again.

Also Iâ€™m thinking of starting with CT and adding seaweed to my diet. But I would like to know what to expect. How can this affect my thyroid function - will I need to make more adjustments to my medication?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-16T17:58:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-103593>

If you do exercise make sure it is short burst high intensity. The fat can be added to limit your cravings. Everyone is a bit different there with fats because their inner mitochondrial membrane is in a state of flux due to the problems at cytochrome 1 and 2 you will soon learn about in the next blog.

### Andrea — 2013-12-25T07:31:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-104231>

Thanks, dr. K!
I was thinking of Doug McGuff's and John Little's concept from "Body by science": doing 5 exercises working the largest muscle groups, lifting 75% of max, as slowly as possible, as long and as many times as possible, with shortest possible break between the exercises.

I haven't started training yet though. I decreased my T4 med and increased my T3 med last week and something tells me that I should wait a couple of weeks until I feel whether the new doses are ok or not.

But I have started with CT. I found the face dipping somewhat frustrating and jumped ahead to ice on my chest and belly wearing a compression shirt, which is sort of interesting. The first few seconds are uncomfortable, but then it doesn't really bother me. And I tend to fall asleep after a while...

Anyway, I have made the conclusion that my BAB isn't completely evil. I thought I didn't have much cravings before, on my low carb high fat diet, although I chose to eat a piece of good chocolate once in a while (which easily became a whole chocolate bar...), but now... We've had enormous amounts of chocolate and sweets at work before Christmas and I didn't mind sitting with my nose in the midst of it watching everyone else stuff their face - didn't feel any desire whatsoever to taste. And I helped my family make sweets for Christmas and I prefered to wash my hands from chocolate rather than licking them...
Not bad for being me!

### Andrea — 2014-01-03T17:54:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-104662>

**CT is so cool!**
I've been freezing most of the time for several years, always wearing warm clothes and turning the heat up as much as possible. But now...
Now... After CT, I walk around at home wearing only a bathrobe (no socks!). I feel warm. My skin is cold and my feet are more like lumps of ice, but I **feel** warm. When I take a normal warm shower I have to turn the water down about 5Â°C compared to before - it just feels too hot. The only exception is my head, which still likes it a bit warmer.
And I've hung my winter jacket in the wardrobe and wear my rain coat when I'm outdoors (ok, it's not very cold; the temperature is slightly above freezing, but still).

Awesome!

### caroline — 2014-01-03T23:53:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-104680>

You are making great progress! you must be so proud! ...
Do you feel your thyroid improving? Has it kicked up a notch??

### Andrea — 2014-01-04T06:53:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-104709>

Most of all I'm so happy! Not feeling cold all the time is just amazing!
At work, I'm now one of the lightest dressed people - it's been completely the other way around for several years. My collegues used to wonder if I was a cat or a reptile, considering how warm it's been in my office - now my room is one of the coolest.

However, I'm not sure I sense much difference from my thyroid. If I did, I wouldn't know why, unfortunately. I changed the doses of my meds a couple weeks ago (lowered T4 med and increased T3 med), and that will take a couple more weeks before I can tell if it was right or wrong. And perhaps it was foolish to also start adding kelp to my diet at the same time, but yet it felt foolish not to...

### Andrea — 2014-01-15T19:38:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-105776>

"Finally", the temperature outdoors has dropped to below 0C. The other day it was -8C in the morning (a quick drop from +8-ish the day before), so I took my winter coat on when I went to work. I ended up keeping the coat unbuttoned and removed my hat; it was unnecessarily warm.
Thanks to the relatively cold temperature outside and my generally not too warm clothing, I feel a bit cold much of the day. Not shivering, but close. I used to hate feeling cold like this and I'd put on more warm clothes, but now... I've sort of convinced myself that it's good for me, so I'm ok with it.
But I'm more hungry than usual, and I can't stop eating when my food is warm...

However, the last couple days I haven't been able to make myself do any cold showers. I've tried, but it just felt cold and wrong and I backed out quickly.
And my sleep has just got worse and worse. I'm tempted to bring a frozen leg of lamb to bed and hit my head with it (besides, who would say no to breakfast in bed?)...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-15T20:02:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-105777>

stay the course........

### Inger — 2014-01-15T20:34:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-105782>

Rerun,I wonder if you live close to me? =) Here in Finland doctors does not make many labs either... very old fashioned. But You can go to a private doc, and there you get everything you want, you just have to pay. That is great. My private doc did not knew much either..lol but she gave me all the labs I asked for. They can cost anything from 15 € to over 100 € / lab depending on what it is.

I love that I do not freeze like I used to either, I also was always cold before I started CTing.

I have those days too when it is so cold in my home and outside.. and I just cannot do CT! I mostly do anyways but I take care to be warm first, so I do some vacuuming or some other work first. Or, I take a hot shower lol

I am glad you did the BAB, I love them now - and me too was not a breakfast eater at all. Today I had 400 g raw ground grassfed beef + fat for breakfast... uh IDK I had 300 g first but the after a while I wanted more! So I ate it all... I cannot believe how much I can eat. But I feel great! So I guess I needed it?

Too bad about your sleep. Do you take magnesium? You could take a frozen ice-gelpack and put under a towel under your head on your pillow... maybe it would help? You sure have many wifi signals around you if you live in a city apartment... I have been looking for houses on the countryside too lately -for rent, but it is really not easy to find a good one - always some antennas or stuff too close. I feel there are so many antennas here now it is ridiculous! I think the best thing is to buy land, and a big piece! and then build an own house. yeah. That is the safest thing.. a home with no freaking smart meter

If you want to do some exercise maybe try some yoga or dance, they sure will not hurt you

### Andrea — 2014-01-16T20:51:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-105935>

Inger,
Thanks for your post!

I'm close, but not completely. Sweden.
It's not so easy to find a private doctor who would do the labs of my choice here. If they are connected to the "Social Insurance Agency", which nearly all are, I am not allowed to pay for tests myself and they will only do the labs they think are necessary. And the only two doctors I've found who weren't connected to the agency weren't allowed to do more than "counseling" and referring patients to specialists, who again will be connected to the agency. It's a nightmare, more or less.
Quite some thyroid patients from Sweden travel to Finland to see a good doctor, by the way...

As for the BAB, I have really started to like it! And I could easily eat more and more now. I actually tried a grassfed hamburger (+ quite a lot coconut oil and tallow) for breakfast the other day, and ended up eating 500g. I only fry my burgers for 45 seconds on each side, so the meat is still raw, under a nicely flavored crust.
When I eat eggs with shrimps I don't end up eating THAT much. Usually about 150g shrimps and 3 eggs, sometimes I add an avocado as well. Plus quite a lot coconut oil. Macadamia mayo once in a while.

As for sleeping, yes, I take a teaspoon of magnesium citrate in the evening. More than that wouldn't do any good...
The part about frozen lamb leg in my previous post was a joke. There's one regularly occurring moment that I feel uncomfortably cold, and that's when I go to bed and try to sleep. It passes after a while, but thereâ€™s no way I'd cuddle a frozen leg or ice-gelpack when going to sleep. A hot water bottle, perhaps...

Oh yeah, ridiculous amounts of antennas and wifi everywhere around me in my apartment. Unfortunately, it's not better at work. I have a 3G tower max 100m from my office window. And there are DECT antennas and other nasty stuff in all directions. Unfortunately, I really like my job, the place and the people there, so I really don't want to change. If only I could find a nice place in the forest soon...

By the way, I took a nice, long warm shower before CT today and it did feel better than the previous couple days. Thanks!

### Andrea — 2014-01-18T07:05:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rerun-here-i-am-and-now-what.9116/post-106101>

I visited my dental hygienist yesterday...

Whole my adult life these visits have been painful and they took forever, although my teeth weren't in too bad a shape. I went 1-2 times a year; the frequency correlated to the pain-time. I also used to use HUGE amounts of fluoride (and my thyroid issues came as a surprise...) to stop the spreading of caries.

Now, I've been more or less fluoride free for 3 years. Only use a bamboo toothbrush (no toothpaste) and dental floss.
Since it's been 2 years since my last visit, they reserved an hour.
The hygienist took the usual x-rays and checked them, checked my teeth and gums visually, removed tartar (which I only had a little of on my lower jaw, on the back side of my front teeth) and did some polishing. All in all it took 15 minutes and the hygienist sighed that there was nothing more she could do for me.
She suggested that, if I keep brushing my teeth so well , I come back in 18-24 months.

I'm not too unhappy...

Well, I suppose that washing my mouth with coconut oil would make the surface of my teeth more "glossy", and doing it before eating would probably make it more difficult for stuff to stick = less plague. But as for the antibacterial properties: not all bacteria is evil!

Maybe just by eating virtually no carbs at all keeps the mouth more or less free from the "nasty" bacteria anyway (and there are less things for the bacteria to produce acids and nasty stuff from)? Just like what you eat affects the fauna/flora in the gut?
