here's a program that will change your music to 432hz for you. I'm going to get it and do batch conversions on my itunes
[http://returnto432.com/](http://returnto432.com/)

### Ricardo — 2014-01-13T19:06:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-105560>

I remember going to seaside wooded areas in Nova Scotia:

Listening to the sounds of the carbon based lifeforms existing harmoniously in symbiosis is very grounding and satisfying.

The sound of leaves rustling gently in the wind, forest creatures climbing and scampering about, the incoming waves splashing on the rocks....I think we are hard-wired to feel good in this kind of sound-scape.

I like a lot of musics, but nature has an attraction that is undeniable.

### SeaHorse — 2014-01-13T19:48:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-105565>

I totally agree Ricardo.

Me too. This kind of music (nature) is kind of sacred to me and opens up a different world

I wonder if that is what my sheep are listening to when they lie so silent on the pasture enjoying... something.

I do know how I cannot fall to sleep well if I do not hear the nature - the waterfall.. and the wind in the trees. I never understood how people can sleep with closed window

### Da-mo — 2014-01-19T02:13:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-106170>

A Momentary Lapse of Reason is my favourite Floyd album.

I've seen lots of big acts live but the one I really regret missing happened as a 1 time only concert only an hour from me. The Auckland Philharmonic asked Serj Tankian, composer and System of a Down front man, to do a concert with them. Check these clips out from that live performance and notice that the crowd is not who you would normally see at a symphony.
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Wig6u3ExQM8](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Wig6u3ExQM8) The Charade
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mMBCBn66nwo](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mMBCBn66nwo) Baby
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfCiad6Tm\_E](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QfCiad6Tm_E) Gate 21
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GEkQR9Wzock](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GEkQR9Wzock) Empty Walls

BTW, the rock versions are awesome too if you like it better that way. e.g. [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bobdhszyDzk](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bobdhszyDzk) The Charade Rock Version

"I do believe
You are strong in forming violence
We are strong performing silence
We are deceived
By all the toys of our convenience
Opening our hearts to their brilliance"

. . . . aint that the truth!!

### caroline — 2014-01-20T00:32:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-106231>

I love all of this.... Thanks Dr. K. and Sandy and Nicole. All of this enriches your soul - takes you to another level - a deeper level.

I was thinking that I missed way too much because I spent too much time working - but in reality, I spent my days talking/listening to hundreds of people - they enriched my soul massively. I used to choose the CDs for the restaurant and it was always a topic of conversation - what is playing? can I get it at the music shop next door?
So - I had to choose music that most people would love and enjoy and we could talk about.

Do you guys know Gordon Lightfoot?

I love B.B. King... one of the very favorite restaurant CDs - it was hugely important to have an engaging backdrop.....

The soundscape to my life is so different from you guys...... we spent a lot of time in the Bahamas with a bunch of guys \[a band\] who owned a supper club .....we spent as much time as possible at the club listening to them and dancing.

Sun, sand and sea and the wonderful Bahamian culture and music. So - my life has been so different ... as all of ours have been. Fascinating ......or am I just easily amused???

### Da-mo — 2014-01-20T01:04:26+0200

I have read that we're kinda hard wired for certain harmonics and sound progressions that are fibonacci ratios related - in the same way that we are visually wired to find certain proportions more appealing. Even the timing of music, the spaces between the notes show relationships to these numbers. Even down th the ratio of one turn of DNA versus the length it takes to make the turn. All through the natural universe it seems.

### Da-mo — 2014-01-21T09:55:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-106438>

Just been reading 'Energy and Epigenetics 4: Light, Water and Magnetism' and I was reminded of another couple of songs by Serj Tankian from his 'Imperfect Harmonies' album.

"Peace Be Revenged" references to rain, mitochondria and disease and disregard for nature [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DRfYgZl3Z9U](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DRfYgZl3Z9U)
Lyrics for Peace Be Revenged [http://songmeanings.com/songs/view/3530822107858844955/](http://songmeanings.com/songs/view/3530822107858844955/)

_Once we used to hear,
The light footsteps of dear,
Now we have no fear
And in turn we'll disappear,
We have built our lives,
Without nature in mind,
Slowly we've become the disease
But we won't go_

"Electron" references to electrons, protons, rain, sunlight and lightning [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TnwZOBswzQw](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TnwZOBswzQw)
Lyrics for Electron [http://songmeanings.com/songs/view/3530822107858844951/](http://songmeanings.com/songs/view/3530822107858844951/)

_We follow the ever-falling rain,
Electrons protons fight again,
So, we run,
We follow the ever-rising sun,
With feelings of all becoming one,
Then, we fly, . . . . ._

_"When people ask me what religion I am, I always say, see that tree outside, go ask the trees the same thing, you know? I believe very firmly that indigenous populations had a really good, intuitive understanding of why we're here. And we're trying to gain that same understanding through psychology and intellect in modern civilization."_ \- Serj Tankian

I just find his music so inspirational, just like JK's work. Anyway, 'EE4: Light Water and Magnetism' reminded me of these songs . . . . .

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-24T11:58:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-106896>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=106890)
>
> nice Mike - thanks .....Have you seen them live?

Yes, I have. It was a pretty good show.

There's a video of Sting from The Police singing "Can She Excuse My Wrongs" by the Elizabethan lutenist John Dowland. It's quite powerful done in updated style with multiple over-dubbed vocals and the lute player who accompanies him, Karamazov, doing something like a guitar-solo on his lute. Maybe Dowland would have approved? It is treating his music as part of contemporary culture rather than pickling it in aspic in an (anyway conjectural) authentic performance. And people have said that Dowland was like the rockstar of his day!

\[video=youtube;nntri9OfaRY\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nntri9OfaRY\[/video\]

> Can she excuse my wrongs with Virtue's cloak?
> Shall I call her good when she proves unkind?
> Are those clear fires which vanish into smoke?
> Must I praise the leaves where no fruit I find?
>
> No no: where shadows do for bodies stand,
> Thou may'st be abus'd if thy sight be dim.
> Cold love is like to words written on sand,
> Or to bubbles which on the water swim.
>
> Wilt thou be thus abused still,
> Seeing that she will right thee never?
> If thou canst not o'ercome her will
> The love will be thus fruitless ever.
>
> Was I so base, that I might not aspire
> Unto those high joys which she holds from me?
> As they are high , so high is my desire:
> If she this deny, what can granted be?
>
> If she will yeld to that which reason is,
> It is Reason's will that Love should be just.
> Dear make me happy still be granting this,
> Or cut off delays if that die I must.
>
> Better a thousand times to die,
> Than for to live thus still tormented:
> Dear, but remember it was I
> Who for thy sake did die contended

### Nick — 2014-01-29T10:01:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-107267>

Hmmm, Pink Floyd...... First heard PF The Wall when I was introduced to it by my very cool, dope smoking, hippy, buddhist uncle in the mid eighties. I had not heard anything like it and it quickly accelerated my love of music in a new direction. I grew up listening to music, my parents wouldn't let us have a TV till I was about 10 so instead we would always listen to the radio or play records. This has carried on to the current day where at home we watch very little TV but play music most of the time. My 3yo and 7yo react spontaneously and instantly whenever a track comes on that they like. They can't help but break into movement dance and motion, it is awesome to watch and a lot of fun.

Over the last 20 years my ever growing hobby has been to build a better stereo to listen to music on. I have been upgrading and improving steadily where I am now at the point where it is an amazing experience. I am not an audiophile and I get annoyed with the sort of people that will analyse a sound to death, all I ever wanted to do was play the music that I like up loud and to be able top bask in a sea of music, clean and undistorted. I wish I had played Jude some music when she was over but that connection hadn't been made at the time.

After reading JK's insights into sounds and frequencies and health I am certainly looking at my hobby in a new light. Couldn't tell you what that is but I am watching this space with a lot of interest as Jack continues with the journey. In the meantime I never ever imagined that this forum would be so into Pink Floyd, that is crazy and can't be a coincidence????

### prAna303 — 2014-02-05T23:16:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-108566>

Yes, always.

Love is nothing we loose, it is a form of resonance that can not be silenced. When music is used as a therapy, natural part of memory is restored.

I think we have a wrong way of seeing time, mostly because we do not have enough energy to expand our view. Loosing someone is just letting someone go.

> Now let me tell you one very basic yoga structure of your being.
> Just as physicists think that the whole consists of nothing but electrons, electric energy, yoga thinks that the whole consists of nothing but sound electrons. The basic element of existence for yoga is sound because life is nothing but a vibration. Life is nothing but an expression of silence. Out of silence we come and into silence we dissolve again. Silence, space, nothingness, nonbeing, is your innermost core, the hub of the wheel. Unless you come to that silence, to that space where nothing else remains except your pure being, liberation is not attained. This is the yoga framework.

Now VAN....he's quite the combo of cerebral fireworks and a crazy good voice. He is my all time favourite for long car trips. Driving back and forth between Newfoundland and Vermont my girls knew that we had to listen to Van Morrison at least once every hour (we were in the car for a couple of days). I love listening to all the changes and experiments he's made with his music over the years.. Willie and Ray....perfect! All of them are amazing collaborators with other musicians....something that happens when you have a creative, generous heart.

OK I think we all need to have a meet up just so we can listen to music together!

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-22T04:05:13+0200

\[

> quote="JoeBranca, post: 111605, member: 784"\]Nice! Coincidentally I just found out BT (I linked a couple of his pieces above) put out an album in 432 Hz in 2012. It is electronic and beatless but with classically informed piano... Also just the first few minutes embraces in a warm, embryonic way

I liked that too..
Joe...do you know Steve Roach's music? I studied in Arizona with his wife Linda Kohanov and we got to hear a lot of his music in his studio. So, I looked up what this type of music is called and this is what I came up with.....tribal ambient, space, new age electronic..what a mouthful!

### JoeBranca — 2014-02-22T06:22:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-111665>

That's great! I'm a little bit aware of Steve Roach's music, I know he's worked with Fripp and I think Steve Reich, with whom I'm a little more familiar. I fell into new territories like ambient, electronic, avant garde, minimalist, & free jazz back in college once I realized all kinds of musical idioms were out there, hand crafted from so many other exotic places other than what we grow up with.

What struck me is not so much the alternative spiritual underpinnings in a lot of these forms, but the overall modernist approach to a deeper attention and sense of space within sound, the actual notes and space between the notes becoming a basic architecture defining some intersting, implied environments. Music that's sort of \*with\* you rather than playing \*at\* you. There's a lot of pretenders in these genres too, but I really like to camp out sometimes in the works of the true artists.

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-22T15:05:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-111682>

Yes...I make a big distinction between the kind of mechanical, endless rainforest musak that plays in the naturapaths office and people who actually listen to water and do something amazing with the sounds. It's a good way to choose yoga teachers too....do they play top-40 new age or are you listening to real artists.

On the other hand, a couple of years ago I was listening to a lot of recordings with binaural beats and/or particular brain wave patterns. One of my favorites was simply the sound of rain...from Immrama institute...no flutes or synth. needed. It was really effective at changing my thinking because of the range of embedded frequencies. It wasn't creative like Steve Roach's music though. I like his CD Serpent's Lair the best. Dr Weil (not my favourite MD in many respects) put out a sound therapy CD set that had a lecture and then a selection of tunes...that was great music too.

Still, nothing's better than being outside and opening your ears. I heard a totally new sound last week. We've had a lot of snow and the sound of it moving off the roof is familiar it kind of rumbles and whooshes like a giant zipper...this time I was out in a fairly steep sloping field and the snow was very, very sandy, light about two feet deep and hadn't been touched. The vibration of my walking through the snow set off a fissure opening in the top layer further down the slope... the sound was like a snake hissing through dry grass but deeper....my dog completely lost his mind and couldn't place where it happened or what happened. I didn't see the crack opening until the second time it happened and I thought is was so cool....a new sound from snow that I'd never heard before.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-03T10:45:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-113149>

Here's another folksong. It begins with a lark, as all good folksongs should. It has a girl binding her golden hair. There's an outlaw, who's being hunted, including by the girl's family. She escapes to the wilds to go to him, but on the way gets caught in a storm and drowned:

> Well morn has come, she rose and fled
> From her cruel kin and home;
> And bright the wood and rosy red
> And the tumbling torrent's foam.
> But the mist came down and the tempest roared,
> And all around did destroy;
> And a pale, drowned bride met Renardine,
> On the mountains of Pomeroy.

Well ... it wouldn't be a folksong if the girl didn't get drowned.

### caroline — 2014-03-05T07:13:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-113515>

crap ..... the girl with the beautiful hair drowns.....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T19:52:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-113624>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=113515)
>
> crap ..... the girl with the beautiful hair drowns.....

Here's another folksong played by the Hot Rats. This one is called "Step it out Mary".

It's about a girl with long and golden hair. Her father tries to marry her to a rich man, but she's in love with a soldier … so she drowns herself.

This is getting to be a familiar theme …

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T20:02:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-113627>

Here's a jollier folksong. I think the words were collected by Cecil Sharp in Norfolk but the tune in Somerset.

Here the girl says to the soldier:

> But if you come round to my mummy's house, when the moon shines bright and clearly,
> I will come down and let you in, and my mummy shall not hear me.

And no-one drowns.

Here's the same tune used in a piece by Ralph Vaughan Williams. Quite different in feel.

OK, maybe time for another video. Here's a recitation for a change. Alec Guinness reads the first part of T. S. Eliot's _The Wasteland_ … a "heap of broken images" …

> —Yet when we came back, late, from the Hyacinth garden,
> Your arms full, and your hair wet, I could not
> Speak, and my eyes failed, I was neither
> Living nor dead, and I knew nothing,
> Looking into the heart of light, the silence.
> _Öd’ und leer das Meer._

… and we also have "Madame Sosostris, famous clairvoyante" which should please Audrey.

### NeilBB — 2014-03-14T19:05:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-115417>

For you Pink Floyd fans. One of Roger Waters best solo songs, very appropriate for our modern dilemmas...

We watched the tragedy unfold
We did as we were told
We bought and sold
It was the greatest show on earth
But then it was over
We ohhed and aahed
We drove our racing cars
We ate our last few jars of caviar
And somewhere out there in the stars
A keen-eyed look-out
Spied a flickering light
Our last hurrah
And when they found our shadows
Grouped around the TV sets
They ran down every lead
They repeated every test
They checked out all the data on their lists
And then the alien anthropologists
Admitted they were still perplexed
But on eliminating every other reason
For our sad demise
They logged the only explanation left
This species has amused itself to death
No tears to cry no feelings left
This species has amused itself to death

### Josh — 2014-03-14T20:11:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-115430>

Eliot and Gurdjieff were contemporaries....

> "Dear Grandfather! Your thorough explanations of various episodes that occurred during the process of existence of the three-brained beings on the planet Earth have given me a clear idea and satisfactory understanding of the surprising strangeness of their psyche; nevertheless, the question still arises in me about one particularity of theirs which I cannot yet understand at all and which, even taking into account the strangeness of their psyche, appears to me illogical. My thoughts keep returning to this perplexing question and were even concentrated on it during the holy sacrament in the djamdjampal.
> "From all your explanations about the process of existence of these three-brained beings, I have seen very clearly that although most of them, during their responsible existence, particularly since the third transapalnian perturbation, have a purely automatic Reason, yet even with this automatic Reason they can deliberate so well that they are able to determine, more or less exactly, many of the laws of Nature on their planet, in accordance with which they can even invent certain things on their own.
> "At the same time, parallel with this, reminders of that particularity proper to them alone—that is, the periodic need
> to destroy one another's existence—run like a crimson thread through all your tales.
> "And so, dear Grandfather, I cannot understand at all how it happens that in spite of having existed for such a long time, they have never become aware and even now remain unaware of the horror of this property of theirs.
> "Don't they really ever see that of all the horrors that could possibly exist in the whole of the Universe, these processes are the most terrible? And don't they ever ponder on this matter so that they might become aware of this horror and find some means of eradicating it?
> "Please, Grandfather, tell me why this is so, and which aspects among all
> those making up the strangeness of their psyche are the causes of this
> particularity of theirs?"

> " 'Plague and cholera are in any event nobler than human honesty, for people with a conscience can at least live at peace with them. '

> "Certainly there is something not quite right here.
> "But if nothing could be done by that Being who has now the Reason of
> the sacred 'Podkoolad' and is one of the first assistants of our Endlessness in
> the government of the world, namely, the Very Saintly Ashiata Shiemash—if
> even he could do nothing for the beings of that planet, what can we say, we
> whose Reason is scarcely higher than that of ordinary beings?
> "Do you remember how the Very Saintly Ashiata Shiemash, in his
> deliberations under the title of 'The Terror of the Situation,' said.
> " 'If it is still possible to save the beings of the Earth, Time alone can do it.'
> "As for us, we can only say the same thing in regard to this terrible
> property of theirs we have just been speaking of, that is, their periodic need to
> destroy each other's existence.
> "For the moment, we can only say that if this property of the terrestrial
> beings is to disappear from that unfortunate planet, it will be through Time
> alone, either under the guidance of a Being with very high Reason or thanks
> to certain exceptional cosmic events.

George Gurdjieff, _Beelzebub's Tales to his Grandson , chapter 43

> "The sole means now of saving the beings of the planet Earth would be to
> implant in their presence a new organ, an organ like kundabuffer, but this
> time having such properties that every one of these unfortunates, during the process of his existence, should constantly sense and be aware of the inevitability of his own death, as well as of the death of everyone upon whom his eyes, or attention, rest.
> "Only such a sensation and such an awareness could destroy the egoism now so completely crystallized in them that it has swallowed up the whole of their essence, and at the same time uproot that tendency to hate others which flows from it—the tendency that engenders those mutual relationships which are the chief cause of all their abnormalities, unbecoming to three-brained beings and maleficent for them and for the whole of the Universe. "

Chpater 47_

There's a version of _The Love Song of Alfred J. Prufrock_ at the free site Librivox.

The reader maybe goes a bit towards over-emphasis and over-characterisation, but it is a free volunteer reading after all:

[https://librivox.org/short-poetry-collection-118/](https://librivox.org/short-poetry-collection-118/)

There's actually a recording of Eliot himself reading Prufrock around. Eliot reads his own poetry in a very flat and rather chanting way, though:

### Josh — 2014-03-15T17:34:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-115614>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=115604)
>
> Headpiece stuffed with straw ... sounds like a dietitian.

> LOL Sure does. And speaking of that, anybody in US ever happen to catch Joy Bauer on NBC in the AM? Saw her today. She looks more like an emaciated skeleton all the time, and this is what passes for a "national nutritional expert" nowadays. She was counting some calories again for us this AM...

> To quote Eliot, quoting Joseph Conrad, "The Horror. The Horror..."

Perhaps we have not quite reached the Kurtzian stage or maybe we have passed it, I see us as the mate in Conrad's "Youth" sailing away from the smoldering, soon to explode ship in his "first command" a dinghy with a jury-rig. He waxes rhapsodic about being "in command" for the first time against a backdrop of the sinking ship. He sees it as an adventure, yet it is colored by both the loss of the ship and cargo and the strangeness of the colonial world he sails into. He notes that "Youth" colors all of these things favorably.....Perhaps we are all sailing our lifeboats and dreaming that we are in our first "command" while the mothership smolders and then explodes in the background......Unfortunately, there does not seem to be any warm port for us to sail into with new adventures awaiting us........In some ways, this "Youth" may actually come after the Kurtzian stage, perhaps WWI was really the Kurtzian moment in modern history.....

### SeaHorse — 2014-03-15T20:21:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-115653>

Last year I started a lengthy thread about what it felt like to be on the metaphorical Titanic.....the thread kind of went off the rails (mixed metaphors).

I ended up feeling that we really need to build our own lifeboats ...a terrifying, lonely prospect, yet potentially able to sustain a new kind of life...while the crowd on the mother ship faced certain drowning.

There is a possibility of these single lifeboats forming a flotilla......which is how I see the forum and the blogs. ............I hope there is some sense within this imagery!!

### NeilBB — 2014-03-15T20:59:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-115659>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=115653)
>
> Last year I started a lengthy thread about what it felt like to be on the metaphorical Titanic.....the thread kind of went off the rails (mixed metaphors).
>
> I ended up feeling that we really need to build our own lifeboats ...a terrifying, lonely prospect, yet potentially able to sustain a new kind of life...while the crowd on the mother ship faced certain drowning.
>
> There is a possibility of these single lifeboats forming a flotilla......which is how I see the forum and the blogs. ............I hope there is some sense within this imagery!!

I like that very much. I have often myself envisioned modern civilization as a huge ocean liner not necessarily crashed yet, but venturing aggressively and brazenly into enemy territory. (Mankind's current war on nature). The dilemma is that the large ship can still protect us today from the harsher elements of nature (advantages of civilization) in ways that little lifeboats cant. The bad news is that the ocean liners course is foolishly dangerous now and it can only be a matter of time before it sinks.

The current answer seems to maybe involve not little independent lifeboats, but maybe a nimble band of speedboats that stays in the wake of the liner, checking in with it at times, trying to influence the liner's captain, if possible. And always keeping an eye out for inhabitable islands to steer towards when the liner finally does start to really take on water.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-16T10:29:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-115741>

Here's a YouTube video of Richard Burton reading _The Rime of the Ancient Mariner_:

> It is an ancient Mariner,
> And he stoppeth one of three.

I expect the Aussies here will be saying, "Sounds like an England wicket keeper, mate!"

I think Coleridge had been reading writings of the mystics, maybe Jakob Böhme, or someone like that, and had a sense of everything being connected. The point seems to be that the mariner needlessly kills the albatross and somehow because of that everything goes wrong.

He finishes the poem coming back to that connectedness ... and love:

> He tries to finish
> He prayeth best, who loveth best
> All things both great and small;
> For the dear God who loveth us,
> He made and loveth all.

[http://www.poetryfoundation.org/poem/173253](http://www.poetryfoundation.org/poem/173253)

### caroline — 2014-03-24T09:24:50+0200

### prAna303 — 2014-03-24T10:10:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-117554>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=117551)
>
> Peter and Josh .... are you good dancers? do you light up your space?

In my own eyes, just someone who is scratching the surface in dance.

In all of my years of clubbing, dance has been my identity, this is where i can expand myself. From early years of breakdance to house music and hip hop, i have done it all. Not to brag but if i enter a dancefloor i stand out, yes. I take space, i resonate with the music, i connect with the ones that want to. The things with dance in modern days, say at nightclubs is that men get a little annoyed, girls take that side or the one where they gather up with me. Today you should just be part of the herd when dancing, but if you are dance in your soul you must stand out.

For many years, dj´s have been calling me to go out on that special night, they want me there because i am a reason for them to make art...

Not everyone will understand the feeling i get when i get a chance to let go... The harder, the darker side of techno is my deepest passion ever.

Live, not so good sound but one of the females today that delivers something that resonates with me.

### prAna303 — 2014-03-29T11:28:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-118645>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=118552)
>
> [http://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs...want-on-the-dance-floor-according-to-science/](http://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/wonkblog/wp/2014/03/24/what-women-want-on-the-dance-floor-according-to-science/)

That was an painful article for me... Talk about missing so many keypoints in the game between man and woman. For me, those two videos show nothing of dance, more of weird science. I showed it to three girls lats night, just to test and they all said, what the hell is that... Dance is like a way to connect and let go, to share and gather as a tribe, no matter the level you are at.

Later on we watched a classic movie about a culture in USA mainly, crumping and clowning. Guess what, they all said that it resonated so much with them and if they would see it live they would pick those guys any day over the ones that do not dance.

Like that dude Einstein said: Dancers are the athletes of god...

### prAna303 — 2014-03-30T09:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-118776>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=118757)
>
> I am givin' you all a two month heads up.........after the May webinar is over...........I got a funny feeling this will be popular: The first four minutes of this video.........are going to parallel this webinar.........and then at 4:00.............BOOM. Next level entered.
>
>
> That is all I am gonna say...................

Nice, a little game of teasing...

For me i get a lot of reactions when listening to this, same with many pieces of Vangelis...

The first part is slowly building up, opening my mind, if played LOUD on a good system. When i do this, vinyl, really loud, the sound picture is expanding, my room becomes extra large. This is like a new life starting, slowly building up.

The second part, where the drums comes alive, is becoming alive, because the usage of tones, vibrations and connecting all senses. The hand in sync with every instrument, like how your body works in sync if you are alive. Drums have been with us from the beginning i think, a way to not only communicate but also activate...

This is building up, good, i need some brain food for my summernights...

### prAna303 — 2014-03-30T10:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-118781>

And how it feels to make music by yourself... Have you ever tried it? Holding a guitar, just touching the strings, slowly start play, feel the things happening inside you. When a chord is played, it resonates directly with you and you are able to activate your ability to create energy... I think this is why we tend to use music for memories... The vibrations that penetrate skin, ears, eyes and also the direct electrical actions that is produced in us.

We are build of water, how does sound and touch travel thru water?

When i do my freediving a get some really strong feelings of life. Sounds get more vibrant, light gets more rich tone to it. When i touch stuff in the water it feels more, it melts into my skin.

Like i always thought, piano is the quantum computer that all computer geeks tries to manufacture today... I think, so strongly, we must go analog style again.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-01T10:47:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-119184>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=118849)
>
> Once in a small piece of art we can see how pictures, music and emotions sync...

Talking of different forms working together brings Richard Wagner to mind. He was all about the total experience, wasn't he? He had massive reservations about the opera of his day, and I don't think even liked or used the term "opera". He saw himself as trying to devise a new art-form. The music and the words and the visuals (he actually had maniacally detailed written instructions for stage-sets) all had to work together and draw the emotional picture he wanted. Thus in Tristan and Isolde you get this protracted refusal to resolve discordance -- you can _hear_ the emotional intensity.

If I were more musical and my German was better maybe I could sit through a whole Ring Cycle ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-25T17:22:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-123444>

Surely time for some more music ...

Mediaeval songs of love and loss, as sung by the troubadours. This is sung in Occitan; they've tried to reconstruct how the music might have sounded. It's quite haunting.

> That mountain before me
> Is ablaze and burning;
> There I lost my love.
> I sit down and weep. ...

### Albert83BCN — 2014-05-16T10:46:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-127196>

"The visions dancing in my mind
The early dawn, the shades of time
Twilight crawling through my windowpane

Am I awake or do I dream,
The strangest pictures I have seen
Night is day and twilight's gone away

With your head held high and your scarlet lies
You came down to me from the open skies
It's either real or it's a dream
There's nothing that is in between

Twilight, I only meant to stay awhile
Twilight, I gave you time to steal my mind
Away from me.

Across the night I saw your face
You disappeared without a trace
You brought me here, but can you take me back

Inside the image of your light
That now is day and once was night
You lead me here and then you go away.

Twilight, I only meant to stay awhile
Twilight, I gave you time to steal my mind
Away from me.

You brought me here, but can you take me back again?

With your head held high and your scarlet lies
You came down to me from the open skies
It's either real or it's a dream
There's nothing that is in between

Twilight, I only meant to stay awhile
Twilight, I gave you time to steal my mind
Away from me.

Twilight, I only meant to stay awhile
Twilight, I only meant to stay awhile
Twilight, Twilight, Twilight, Twilight."

Summertime, and the livin' is easy
Fish are jumpin' and the cotton is high
Oh, your daddy's rich and your ma is good-lookin'
So hush little baby, Don't you cry

### prAna303 — 2014-08-09T22:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-137402>

The subject of music...

For many years i put away my obsession... I did it almost like in secret, just small bits here and there. Often in private even if i was public, you know the kick without showing it...

I let my own history be silent, lurking, slowly building up... Time? What is that, is it forced with statics, rules and measure points only?

But then again, after four years of escape... Digging deep in my collection, there it was... Wooden, mechanical, oil, craftmanship and pure passion signed by heavy weight. Who wants this today?

The capsule is restored, music is flowing from a gone by era but what great loss has mankind done, throwing away diamonds and pearls for ones and zeroes from an ugly spotify account.

What, what is he talking about?

Think he is saying that the art of audio and analog ways of presenting it is what many is missing today.

So, to give my life and my spirit some extra vivid energy i accept that bad sound, bad presentation is not going to make it. I need the high end world no matter what...

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=144070)
>
> Sounds alot like the Psychedelic Furs, very 80's

Do you know what "Joy Division" refers to? They had their own dark perspective....

> **Joy Division** may refer to:
>
>
> -   The Joy Division, a group of Jewish women forced to work as sex slaves in Nazi concentration camps, as depicted in the 1955 novel _[House of Dolls](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/House_of_Dolls)_
> -   [German camp brothels in World War II](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_camp_brothels_in_World_War_II)
> -   [Kippumjo](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kippumjo), translated variously as "Pleasure Brigade" or "Joy Division," a collective of females maintained by the head of state of [North Korea](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Korea) for the purpose of providing pleasure and entertainment to officials, formed in 1978

As for bonus, watch from 00:40:00. After a while, pop goes the weasel, my definition of special woman. Black dress, dark hair, red lips, fan and a special sense for the beats.

Extra bonus, a little darker, same night.

### Da-mo — 2014-11-13T20:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-147303>

I'm a big Serj Tankian fan - anything from "Elect the Dead" for the rock versions or "Elect the Dead - Symphony" for the classical versions. Such profound lyrics too.

**Electron**

We follow the ever-falling rain
Electrons Protons fight again
So, we run
We follow the ever-rising sun
With feelings of all becoming one
Then, we fly

We fly in our dreams ever so bold
Swimming the seas covered with gold
Behold, proceed
Proceed with the blessing of all the gods
Amidst the time of men at odds
See, believe

Tell me, what it is that we believe
The storm shines bright
Tell me, what it is that we believe
The storm shines bright

Grapevines of thoughts lining the skies
Overwhelmingly of demise
So we sing
We sing to the spirit world so close
Providing our vital life force
Then we breathe

We breathe with the breath of I-Ching
With the spirit that moves through all things
Now, we see
Seeing of all that is alive
Will be the way to survive
Life, released

this is one of those days where I revisit an old, familiar song with fresh ears, and the lyrics speak fresh relevance within my current headspace. This one has hit me as I think about the cards being stacked against us in so many ways with the wrong turns the institutions of science, medicine and technology have taken over decades; _nevertheless_ individual, executable insight is always in reach as long as we are incurably curious. No need to spend a lifetime chasing around gurus for derivative enlightenments.

You can fool yourself
You can cheat until you're blind
You can cut your heart
It can happen

You can mend the wires
You can feed the soul apart
You reach
It can happen to you
It can happen to me
It can happen to everyone eventually

It's a constant fight

No substitute you're born you're dead

Our destinations call

We're not about to lose our pride

### JoeBranca — 2015-04-19T02:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-161689>

I'm on a Herzog tip...

about the 15 year old actress in the film Aguirre, a film that entangles you in the jungle and the river

> "She, like the local Indians, is played by a non-actress, in fact a girl plucked out of a Peruvian high school nearly at random.
>
> **The less she does, the more transcendent her presence. We don't see her die, but as she knows she is dying she looks at her father as if to transfer all her energy to the space immediately surrounding him.** That space does become filled with fantastic life on the raft and all that surrounds it, creating the sequence that ends the film. That scene perfectly compliments the first, where the people are the river bringing their seed to the promised land. In the last, the seed is released and the river itself carries the offspring of the imagination to the treetops."

[http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0068182/reviews-44](http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0068182/reviews-44)

### JoeBranca — 2015-05-29T07:24:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-165763>

For those who can accept the premised elements of electronic dance music - rhythmic repetition, slow growth of themes, dadaist intersplicings, discrete melodic phrases and colorful atmospheric sonics emphasized over melodic structure and lyrics, etc etc -- this record I have believed to be the Dark Side of the Moon of 90's DJ mixes. It really deserves an uninterrupted vinyl spin in darkness, but a youtube play gives the gist. Holds up against time, which is rare for trance from that era.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-06T00:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-171015>

A teaching lesson about numbers: Numbers explain physics better via colors. Colors is all about frequency. Colors for me and hopefully for this group is not about what we see it is about what they are.........packets of vibrational energy. **Consider red light: has a frequency of 436,803,079,680,000 oscillations per second. _That equates to the same energy in a high C note in music_.** That note has an oscillation frequency of 4,096 osc. per sec. Xrays, microwaves, cosmic and gamma rays all have these numbers and colors tied to them.........but when you read them they make no sense to you........but color has a qualia you do get. Your mitochondria in your retina are where your personal magnetic sense is and why this result is present in your test, so the picture you posted has meaning.........to you.

You know what it means to me? Your magnetic sense is no bueno. Do I have a blog on that sense? Yep. Tensegrity 5. Magnetism has the HIGHEST oscillatory rate we know of in nature. Few people know this. It is 18,446,744,073,709,551,616 oscillations per second.........

It is hard for the mind to grasp that many things vibrating that many times in a second........But guess what last week on the cruise.....everyone else did there own thing in Playa Del Carmen on the cruise........and What did I make sure to do? I went into tthe crater where those Oscillations have existed for 65 million yrs.........Patrick Griffin asked me how I expended all the energy I did every day and night on the cruise and not get tired or discharge my mammalian battery. What did I do to prepare? I remained ketotic and at once and day and drank a ton of water and ate only DHA at night........why? Because that water assimilated all those oscillations from the crater to renew me.........I knew I could give a lot.......because I was going to get a lot more in my time in the crater.

For those of you bad at math: That number above is 18 quintillion, 446 quadrillion, 744 trillion, 73 billion, 709 million, 551 thousand, 6 hundred, and 16 oscillations per second of vibrational energy. That my dear is physics 101. Get some for those eye clocks............

### Gagnrad — 2015-07-25T18:56:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-172984>

How about something very upbeat and cheerful?

December, 1963:

According to Wikipedia, this was originally called 1933 and was about the repeal of Prohibition, but "the lyrics were changed at the urgings of Frankie Valli and lyricist Parker to reposition the song as a nostalgic remembrance of a young man's first affair with a woman, and, more specifically, Gaudio's courtship with his wife, Judy Parker"

Michael Franti & Spearhead - Everyone Deserves Music 2003

Michael Franti & Spearhead - The Sound of Sunshine 2010

### Jake Tassell — 2017-09-18T13:20:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-223893>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=105134)
>
> With all this talk about Pink Floyd .... I would like to buy some .... but which ones? which album?
>
> This must sound really lame....
>
> I gather - after a visit to iTunes ..... that a song can be on different albums and have different treatments???
>
> Don't laugh ...... I have had my head buried in Julia Child et all for the last century ...... I have never owned an ipod - preferring live music.
>
> Where should I start? ..... and if you laugh at me - I will know .....

I don't think anyone needs help with their 'musical education'. I really think that's mistake number one.

_You_ know what you like. _You_ know what resonates well with you. If that turns out to be Jim Reeves, Liberace, or The Goombay Dance Band, and everyone laughs at you because of it, so be it, say I.

Why force someone else's square peg idea of what they consider to be 'good music' on yourself.

I personally hate Pink Floyd, but I defend anyone else's right to like Pink Floyd, and I ain't gonna be anyone's enemy _because_ they like Pink Floyd.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-09-18T20:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-223918>

Children of the light.......behold the gods of sonic waves.

Lyrics
Oh, let the sun beat down upon my face
And stars fill my dream
I'm a traveler of both time and space
To be where I have been
To sit with elders of the gentle race
This world has seldom seen
They talk of days for which they sit and wait
All will be revealed

Talk in song from tongues of lilting grace
Sounds caress my ear
And not a word I heard could I relate
The story was quite clear

Oh, baby, I been blind
Oh, yeah, mama, there ain't no denyin'
Oh, ooh yes, I been blind
Mama, mama, ain't no denyin', no denyin'

All I see turns to brown
As the sun burns the ground
And my eyes fill with sand
As I scan this wasted land
Try to find, try to find the way I feel

Oh, pilot of the storm who leaves no trace
Like sorts inside a dream
Leave the path that led me to that place
Yellow desert stream
Like Shangri-la beneath the summer moon
I will return again
As the dust that floats finds you
We're moving through Kashmir

Oh, father of the four winds fill my sails
Cross the sea of years
With no provision but an open face
Along the straits of fear
Oh, when I want, when I'm on my way, yeah
And my feet wear my fickle way to stay

Ooh, yeah yeah, oh, yeah yeah,
But I'm down oh, yeah yeah, oh, yeah
Yeah, but I'm down, so down
Ooh, my baby, oh, my baby
Let me take you there
Come on, oh let me take you there
Let me take you there

> All I see turns to brown
> As the sun burns the ground
> And my eyes fill with sand
> As I scan this wasted land
> Try to find, try to find the way I feel
>
> Oh, pilot of the storm who leaves no trace
> Like sorts inside a dream
> Leave the path that led me to that place
> Yellow desert stream
> Like Shangri-la beneath the summer moon
> I will return again
> As the dust that floats finds you
> We're moving through Kashmir
>
> Oh, father of the four winds fill my sails
> Cross the sea of years
> With no provision but an open face
> Along the straits of fear
> Oh, when I want, when I'm on my way, yeah
> And my feet wear my fickle way to stay
>
> Ooh, yeah yeah, oh, yeah yeah,
> But I'm down oh, yeah yeah, oh, yeah
> Yeah, but I'm down, so down
> Ooh, my baby, oh, my baby
> Let me take you there
> Come on, oh let me take you there
> Let me take you there

Niiiiiicccccceeee.. My mom spoke in tongues, very odd and it freaked me out but I knew it was all for the good. My boyfriend a song of theirs over the weekend called no quarter, it was ok, he just comes up with random stuff while doing air drums..lol. He has energy like a teenager, if a teen boy has sex everyday then that is like my man at 53 years old. He's the best in every way. He is my box of mixed chocolates.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-09-19T01:59:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-223951>

> [Billybats said:](/goto/post?id=223936)
>
> Reeeeaaallly.. I didn't listen to the whole song, didn't understand it. Okay will listen to the whole thing. Thank yooooooou.

The song is about never giving up........giving life No Quarter. Giving your all even when things look grim.

No Quarter lyrics:
Close the door, put out the light.
No, they won't be home tonight.
The snow falls hard and don't you know?
The winds of Thor are blowing cold.
They're wearing steel that's bright and true
They carry news that must get through, oooh
They choose the path where no-one goes.
They hold no quarter, They hold no quarter.
Walking side by side with death
The devil mocks their every step
The snow drives back the foot that's slow
The dogs of doom are howling more
They carry news that must get through
To build a dream for me and you
They choose the path where no-one goes.
They hold no quarter, They ask no quarter.
The pain, the pain without quarter.
They ask no quarter.
Yeah! Without quarter, quarter, yeah!
The dogs of doom are howling more!
I hear the dogs of doom are howling more!

### Da-mo — 2018-01-11T16:37:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-232244>

So . . . . what was the great song of 2017?

My 2nd daughter reckons it was Despacito

but being older and wiser I would place Despacito alongside feel=good songs like the Macarena. these songs and the videos that go with them capture the joy of life and the feeling of being alive. All my children are in their twenties looking forward to the best years of their lives . . . . . . oh to be able to put an old head on young shoulders . . . . . if only to dispel the invincibility of youth
Most of us here (not all) are looking to perpetuate, recover or project that feeling. We are older, we have issues, we have heath problems to solve and we have an urge to fill what time we have left with a quality of life. We want to heal and thrive like we once did without squandering the energy we have like we did in our youth.

For me the song of the last year just has to be RagnBone Man's Human. I challenge you to find anyone who does not know every word of this song and who will not remember it in 10 years time.

Give yourself a beak. You're only human after all . . . . .

### Da-mo — 2018-01-11T17:11:52+0200

BTW,Happy New Year everyone!!! I wish for you all that the year brings you what you need - which is not necessarily what you want. So on a musical note . . . .

I''m not a Stones fan but . . . . if you try sometimes . . . . you just might find . . . . . you get what you need . . . . .

I have a special chess set for this with a board made of sandblasted etched glass and glass chess pieces. I will bequeath it to him one day when I pass from this world. I think I will place a copy of this album with it.

### Da-mo — 2018-01-11T18:56:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-232271>

On my 49th birthday in April 2017 my son picked up a guitar and started playing with it. Some sort of structure formed and he quietly started singing the first verse of a song. At the chorus he momentarily stopped playing the guitar and gestured for people to join in - his sisters (my daughters) joined in the vocal. Totally spontaneous and it was the best birthday present I ever got by FAAAAAARRRR! It wasn't a song they planned to sing for me and I dont think they even sang it to me - but seeing the bond between them in that moment made my whole life worthwhile. This is the song they sang together as brother and sisters ..... it still brings a tear to my eye just recalling that special moment. . ,

The day reminds me of you
The night hides your truth
The earth is a voice
Speaking to you
Take all this pride
And leave it behind
'Cause one day it ends
One day we die

That is your right
But I choose to win
I choose to fight
To fight
'Cause me, I'm rusted and weathered
Barely holding together
I'm covered with skin that peels
And it just won't heal

### Da-mo — 2018-02-24T09:07:17+0200

I'm waking up to ash and dust
I wipe my brow and I sweat my rust
I'm breathing in the chemicals
I'm breaking in, shaping up, then checking out on the prison bus

Enough to make my systems blow
Welcome to the new age, to the new age
Welcome to the new age, to the new age

Hypocritical, egotistical
Don't wanna be the parenthetical, hypothetical
Working onto something that I'm proud of, out of the box
An epoxy to the world and the vision we've lost
I'm an apostrophe
I'm just a symbol to remind you that there's more to see
I'm just a product of the system, a catastrophe
And yet a masterpiece, and yet I'm half-diseased
And when I am deceased
At least I go down to the grave and die happily
Leave the body and my soul to be a part of thee
I do what it takes

\----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
**Greek words for love**

-   _[Agápe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agape)_ (ἀγάπη _agápē_) means "love: esp. charity; the love of God for man and of man for a good God.

-   _[Éros](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek_eros)_ (ἔρως _érōs_) means "love, mostly of the sexual passion.

-   _[Philia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philia)_ (φιλία _philía_) means "affectionate regard, friendship", **usually "between equals".**
-   _[Storge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storge)_ (στοργή _storgē_) means "love, affection" and "especially of parents and children".

\---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
**Restoration of Peter**
Two words used for "love"

.

Nochenka (Night) (Ноченька), folk song

Night ----> that is insufficient translation.
Ноченька ----->> is so much more than just a night

[@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) Ноченька are gallons and gallons of oxytocin
it is better to listen to it with significant other near

love

### 5G Canary — 2019-07-30T22:35:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-275211>

My husband has been listening to “Human” by Rag’n’Bone Man over and over lately. While I love that song too- I have been consistently drawn to his other song appropriately named “Skin.” He wrote it about a deep love that could have been if they hadn’t been from to different worlds.

It has so many double meanings I don’t think the artist even realizes what he wrote. The video and song are beautifully done. To me the song is about what a love affair with Mother Nature might have been like if we hadn’t been forced to live in an artificial world.... causing us to live in these two different worlds.

As he sings...”cause it was almost love....it was almost love.” It definitely reminds me that “it’s not almost”- I still have a choice now. I CHOOSE the LOVE that should have been all along.... I still have “Skin” in the game NOW.

We have a choice and we don’t have to choose these lyrics....

“We bleed ourselves in vain
How tragic is this game?
Turn around, I'm holding on to someone
But the love is gone
Carrying the load, with wings that feel like stone
Knowing that we nearly fell so far now
It's hard to tell
Yeah we came so close, it was almost love
It was almost love, it was almost love
When I heard that sound
When the walls came down
I was thinking about you
About you
When my skin grows old
When my breath runs cold
I'll be thinking about you
About you”

So hold your body, hold your body strong in these winds of life
May I find you, may we sit together when we're gray and old, on cloud nine
This life'll move you with every step outside
it's alright, it's alright my arms are open wide for you
This life'll move you as graceful as a tide
oh it's alright it's alright, loosen the fears that bind you, loosen the fears that bind you

### JoeBranca — 2020-01-08T20:20:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-284344>

Sometimes with music, the story of the creative process is just as beneficial as the actual listening experience. In this case, the artist released an experimental electronic-orchestral album, and the name is Everything You're Searching For is On the Other Side of Fear. Fitting theme to launch me into 2020 as I anticipate the changes and upheaval that comes, not really knowing what the other side will look like. Missteps, challenges, opportunities, growth.

With this particular piece in the youtube clip, I'm impressed with how he did it. Essentially the outcome is as much a discovery for the artist as any listener.

"This was composed in a very unique way - I wrote 4 bars at a time; never listening to the previous 4. By doing this in this fashion (and coming back to it every now and again) it has this unique feeling of contributing to blossom into something new and unexpected (even by me and I wrote it!).
.
"I do these kinds of compositional “thought experiments” as apart of my daily routine and rarely do they amount to something that feels this magical."​

### 5G Canary — 2020-06-03T17:42:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-289996>

[This song has always resonated with me but in a completely different way recently. Most of us can “talk without speaking and hear without listening” ... we all actually have this ability but very few of us use it. We have many senses and it’s about time we start using and trusting in them. We are all connecting whether we acknowledge it or not. Our connection is stronger in our current environment.... zip code matters not just in the environmental way but also in the energy, frequency, vibration and gravity of those in that zip code. This song sung differently resonates the cries of innocence lost in the dark shadows... a sick world that silently has been calling out to us. Silently awakening us to their songs. But most of us are distracted or have kept it silent and like in the lyrics “silence like a cancer grows”.... it’s definitely _Time_ _to_ _Collectively_ make choices to _Change_ our thinking. To choose our thoughts wisely... for the greater good of all. What if it was that easy.... to collectively be silent no more! To Collectively Choose _Love_....](https://youtu.be/u9Dg-g7t2l4)

Words cause our banner, victorious our day. Will silence be promised as violence display? The curse increased we fight the pow'r And live by it by day. Our gods awake in thunderous roars, And guide the leaders hand in paths of glory to the cause.

### 5G Canary — 2020-10-09T19:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-292774>

[@John Schumacher](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/23768/)
Thanks for sharing your favorites too! Kashmir.... I’ve always loved this song too!

**Kashmir** is the northwestern region of the Indian subcontinent. It is also called and known as the Heaven on Earth because of its natural widespread beauty. Until the mid-19th century, the term **Kashmir** geographically denoted only the valley between the Great Himalayas and the Pir Panjal mountain range.

**_Oh, let the sun beat down upon my face

And stars fill my dream
I’m a traveler of both time and space
To be where I have been
To sit with elders of the gentle race
This world has seldom seen
They talk of days for which they sit and wait
All will be revealed_**

Robert Plant once said, “I wish we were remembered for Kashmir [more than Stairway To Heaven](https://auralcrave.com/en/2018/07/15/stairway-to-heaven-why-led-zeppelins-masterpiece-is-actually-a-personal-growth-lesson/)”. I wonder why? I always felt like Robert Plant was literally “**_Planting_**”... planting seeds through his music and collectively we are water those seeds.

According to folk etymology, the **name** "**Kashmir**" means "desiccated land" (from the Sanskrit: Ka = water and shimeera = desiccate). In the Rajatarangini, a history of **Kashmir** written by Kalhana in the mid-12th century, it is stated that the valley of **Kashmir** was formerly a lake.“ Coincidence “**_Plant_**” and “**_Water_**?” The song was brilliantly orchestrated and continues to grow....

Yes’s song.....Sounds like “Yes” you served in the military... thank you for your service! Love the line in the song....” Listen, should we fight forever Knowing as we do know fear destroys?” Fear is the real virus and Love is the cure. When they get us to live in fear we live our lives backwards. Live mirrored is Evil.... fear is the enemy in which we must fight against.

> [View attachment 16738](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/16738/)

[@5G Canary](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22671/) -> _Thank you for your thoughtful response !_
I was fortunate, my name was not pulled during the draft. I was spared. War, hatred, revenge are not in my practice. I've sought after: collaboration (rather than competition), love (rather than dissension), forgiveness (rather than retaliation). I see the divine has weaved a functional path for us to find. I'm currently writing a book. Here's a clip:

Water instructs us best in understanding why _Nature_ designed molecular collaboration. It shows us that water is preoccupied with extensive self-association. Water clathrates pull its positive electrons in and stratifies out its negative electrons. Nature reveals both a push toward surface tension and a cohesive pull, creating a beautiful spherical form. This gives water properties for hydration and solubility, yet it can repel “almost hold at bay” molecules like lipid hydrocarbons, _fatty acids,_ without losing its innate characteristics.

Question: Can we humans learn from water our need for collaborative interdependancy?

Looking Forward...
John

### Dan2 — 2020-12-05T09:21:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-293670>

> [Chantelle said:](/goto/post?id=293668)
>
> This made my day. I love finding new music...

I was really excited finding that band too! I like finding music from countries other than the USA/UK/Canada. And when it's from the '60s onward a lot of the bands were influenced by popular pop and rock bands from the US and England, but they also have the influences of the music from their countries, so it's an interesting combination.

And sometimes funny

This one's hilarious

\_\_\_

There are record labels that put together albums that are compilations of songs from various bands (and full albums by one band too). It's a good way to find new songs and bands.

Soundway
[https://soundwayrecords.bandcamp.com/](https://soundwayrecords.bandcamp.com/)
[https://soundwayrecords.com/](https://soundwayrecords.com/)

Strut
[https://strut.bandcamp.com/](https://strut.bandcamp.com/)
[http://www.strut-records.com/](http://www.strut-records.com/)

Numero Group
[https://numerogroup.bandcamp.com/music](https://numerogroup.bandcamp.com/music) (Ctrl+F search "Various")
Sub-label, Eccentric Soul
[https://eccentricsoul.bandcamp.com/music](https://eccentricsoul.bandcamp.com/music)

Soul Jazz Records
[https://soundsoftheuniverse.com/sjr/browse/c-music/y-/o-all/v-grid/s-hide/p-1](https://soundsoftheuniverse.com/sjr/browse/c-music/y-/o-all/v-grid/s-hide/p-1)

Analog Africa
[https://analogafrica.bandcamp.com/](https://analogafrica.bandcamp.com/)
[https://analogafrica.com](https://analogafrica.com/)

\_\_\_

This one's similar to Os Mutantes.
Marilene - Sinal Vermelho
[https://strut.bandcamp.com/track/sinal-vermelho](https://strut.bandcamp.com/track/sinal-vermelho)

Another good song from that same album
[https://strut.bandcamp.com/track/pra-swingar](https://strut.bandcamp.com/track/pra-swingar)

This band's also similar to Os Mutantes, a little weirder (other songs). Their first album was in 2013.
[https://familyatlantica.bandcamp.com/track/neti-neti](https://familyatlantica.bandcamp.com/track/neti-neti)

Another recent band -- soul and funk
[https://elmichelsaffair.bandcamp.com/album/sounding-out-the-city](https://elmichelsaffair.bandcamp.com/album/sounding-out-the-city)
They also made two albums covering Wu Tang beats and a cover album of Isaac Hayes's Walk On By.

The least obscure recent funk band

I like the poppy African songs with lyrics I don't understand and lots of people singing (I posted some others like that in earlier posts).
[https://soundwayrecords.bandcamp.com/track/bokoor-band-you-can-go](https://soundwayrecords.bandcamp.com/track/bokoor-band-you-can-go)

Jazz with African and Latin influences, "Highlife"
[https://soundwayrecords.bandcamp.com/track/oju-rere](https://soundwayrecords.bandcamp.com/track/oju-rere)

Similar to that, Mulatu Astatke's Ethiopian jazz
[https://mulatuastatke.bandcamp.com/album/new-york-addis-london-the-story-of-ethio-jazz-1965-1975-2](https://mulatuastatke.bandcamp.com/album/new-york-addis-london-the-story-of-ethio-jazz-1965-1975-2)

There's a lot of good dance music influenced by traditional Latin styles
[https://denguedenguedengue.bandcamp.com/track/como-bailar-cumbia](https://denguedenguedengue.bandcamp.com/track/como-bailar-cumbia)

This one sounds like it could've been a hit for Michael Jackson. Or Sly and the Family Stone. From South Africa, 1975.

([https://nextstopsoweto.bandcamp.com...unk-psych-sounds-from-the-townships-1969-1976](https://nextstopsoweto.bandcamp.com/album/next-stop-soweto-vol-2-soultown-r-b-funk-psych-sounds-from-the-townships-1969-1976))

Cheesy Brazilian disco
05\. Robson Robson - Funk-Funk-La
[https://culturesofsoul.com/products...ion-from-rio-to-sao-paulo?variant=27070354889](https://culturesofsoul.com/products/the-brazilian-boogie-connection-from-rio-to-sao-paulo?variant=27070354889)

After the disco some cleansing is needed

### Dan2 — 2020-12-06T15:25:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-293696>

I was listening to his albums and got to that song you posted and thought yeah this is one of the best ones. The chord progression and arrangement sounds like lots of songs from that era but it's good when the little details come together and sound just right, you know. And I like the lyrics being in another language because it adds wonder while I feel like I still understand it some.

I skipped through all of his '70s albums.
(I took the "[https://www](https://www)." out of the links below so it doesn't slow the page down auto-loading too many videos.)

02 Ma E' Un Canto Brasileiro
youtube.com/watch?v=Nj2R2CXJr9k&list=PLbIN9DHtOfK-OC9LDl0ofjVwHMupJm3Rc&index=2
1:35 sounds Os Mutantes-y. And then the contrast with the electric David Bowie-sounding part after 3:00 and it builds like it's going into a heavy chorus but then instead back into the softness. I like how that complements.

05\. Confusione
youtube.com/watch?v=Y3AfI061swo&list=PLbIN9DHtOfK85uoGgjiPGTr3mh7zLt65N&index=5
At 3:05 sounds like a synth cat. They only do that one more time at 4:17. I love finding those moments, like the whistles in Cumbia Que Te Vas de Ronda ([https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...h-my-music-education.9297/page-45#post-292732](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/page-45#post-292732)).

04\. Umanamente Uomo IL Sogno
youtube.com/watch?v=IQQa6OZ8EWs&list=PLbIN9DHtOfK9k9fFdjcC0FSkC8uCHNAjt&index=4
I like the whistling in this too. And the whistling and humming being a little out of tune sometimes. The whistling melody at 2:34 is beautiful. That part's perfect.
I've been relistening to this one lots of times. This one's my favorite. Something about the whistling melodies. This is a good find. Up there with the Os Mutantes 1968 version of Baby.
I like the soft sound of other songs on that album, too -- "Umanamente uomo: il sogno".

A short (0:52) fun one
Gli uomini celesti (Ripresa)
youtube.com/watch?v=ClLwbSdp0nQ

Traditional Central and South American musical styles mixed with psychedelics and electronic effects makes some interesting genre, but I don't know much about it, what its called and the nuances of subgenres. True.
I played a little guitar once, I should play a little again. Music is not like in the old days, as it once was. Spotify, MP3 is not like an LP player, etc. The technology has taken some of the nuances out of music. Made it flatter in a way. Music was often played at 432 Hz, but that is no longer the case, most of the frequencies have been changed and adapted to a "better" technical standard ... frequencies that are easier for the technology to use.

### Dan2 — 2021-09-28T02:09:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-304321>

> [Johan Lindstrøm said:](/goto/post?id=304312)
>
> Anyone have any info??
> { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

The notes opera singers sing are affected by the reference pitch and tuning intonation system. Some pitches are more stressful to the singer's body when using opera technique and some pitches are easier to sing with good technique. The reasoning explaining it gets into the microcosm macrocosm ideas about the human body and the solar system. So maybe when the solar system changes then opera singers can sing better at new pitches, but apparently opera singers have been preferring some pitches more than others since the Renaissance, so I guess the solar system changes slowly. And the pitches being stressful or easier has a lot to do with the intensity of the technique control and volume of opera singers; someone singing jazz or blues or something with more casual technique could sing at A440 for a hundred years and it's not gonna damage their voice, but an opera singer might notice damage to the clarity and control of their voice quickly enough to end their career early. But if the resonance of some pitches and harmonics can harm the physiology of the opera singer because of the intensity of the resonance, maybe a rock singer's voice being amplified and standing in front of the PA would affect their voice quickly (still doesn't matter much though, unless they're singing in a hair metal band where they want to sound like opera technique, because they can just sing with a new voice -- like Bob Dylan's voice. An opera singer's technique and voice is like a representative of an ideal body (especially when it's a fat Italian girl), and so the differences affect their role as a singer and whether they're considered a technically good opera singer of that voice type. If Dylan's ideal is how he's heard, I guess okay, sure, Bob, chain smoke and get in a motorcycle accident and play and sing at any reference pitch you want to and it still sounds good. Or like Mick Jagger on Monkey Man -- be careful about the pitches around your register shifts while making the monkey sounds, Mick; you don't want to sound unprofessional.)

Look at these maybe in this order, or to get your attention the video first.

[https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/rev\_tuning\_hist.html](https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/rev_tuning_hist.html)

[https://r.schillerinstitute.org/fid\_91-96/fid\_911\_jbt\_tune.html](https://r.schillerinstitute.org/fid_91-96/fid_911_jbt_tune.html)

So when middle C is 256 hz, the A can be a different frequency depending what the temperament, intonation system is -- equal temperament, just intonation, Pythagorean etc. With equal temperament, when C's 256, the A's about 430.5 if I remember right.

First she sings at A440 (I think, or some high pitch around there). C256 singing starts at 7:08. The way she breathes in at 9:04 is so good. And 9:49 and 11:19. The way her face and eyes open up at 11:22 onward is beautiful. She's like a cute baby being overwhelmed with herself, with beauty. Such a cutie. From 11:19 on, God, it's so beautiful. I'm in awe of her.

More about reference pitch and opera singers' voices:

[https://larouchepub.com/eiw/public/1988/eirv15n06-19880205/eirv15n06-19880205\_025-renata\_tebaldi.pdf](https://larouchepub.com/eiw/public/1988/eirv15n06-19880205/eirv15n06-19880205_025-renata_tebaldi.pdf)

[https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/rev\_chart.html](https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/rev_chart.html)

[https://larouchepub.com/eiw/public/...0812\_024-revive\_verdis\_tuning\_to\_bring\_ba.pdf](https://larouchepub.com/eiw/public/1988/eirv15n32-19880812/eirv15n32-19880812_024-revive_verdis_tuning_to_bring_ba.pdf)

More videos:
[https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/2007/historic\_videos.html#960529](https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/2007/historic_videos.html#960529)

A bunch of other links:
[https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/articles.html](https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/articles.html)
[https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/revolution.html](https://r.schillerinstitute.org/music/revolution.html)

### JoeBranca — 2023-05-09T18:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-321139>

Since the Tetragrammaton podcast I was reminded how much Rick Rubin is an inspiring human being to me. He facilitates and gets the pieces and players in place to produce something new or fresh. The podcast episode, his initiative to bring together the melanin/POMC world to the neuroplasticity world, is the latest example. The earliest example of him doing this had an impact on me. Actually bigger than I realized, as I'm thinking about it fresh decades later.

When I was 10 or 11, a song on the radio was the only one that provoked me enough to go down to the record store and buy an album for the first time in my life. It was that Aerosmith/Run DMC collab, back around 85 or 86. That was Rick's doing. He proactively brought together two worlds that pop culture at the time said don't go together. My impression is that Rick dug into the 70s archives for a forgotten hard rock hit, and meanwhile he saw the brilliance of the emerging hip hop sounds, and knew they should come together, almost like they could complete each other in some way.

The specific genius in my opinion was Rick either consciously or instinctively knew that the hip hop overhaul of the Aerosmith song would bring to the forefront the essential underlying groove of that sort of classic rock. It was something people felt but didn't really notice underneath all the distorted guitar and screaming, and it got lost in the post disco 80s era. But RR came along and said, No its not a bygone era this is what makes this music so cool, and you can find it everywhere.

All that to say, it wasn't just a song experiment, it was a statement a breakthrough in the "silo" music industry. For the attentive, moments like that really can shape your own ability to perceive differently, be receptive to new ideas no matter the source.

The music video as goofy as it is, really highlights this notion -- the compartmentalized worlds with a wall between them. Only when both sides are provoked enough by each other is there a breakthrough and meeting of minds.

### NikkiYork — 2023-07-27T20:59:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-my-music-education.9297/post-324370>

Watch the movie "the wall" It's great. I mean, it's probably more catering to an angsty kid in puberty, but it should still be good. I need to rewatch it. I was a scared to paralysis adolescent, and for me it was great.

I saw Pink Floyd live when i was 16 , and i didn't like it as much, as I love it in the right setting. It can be very overwhelming, in a good way, and i would say the track depends really on the situation.
I have had one of the best talks and encounters at the 11:11 clock in the middle of the desert at 11;11h, 2010. and they were playing "time". it was at Burning Man, and we traveled out there on our bikes into the unknown, to find a group of people, huddling around that clock, waiting for it to be 11;11. But we didn't know that.

The stars were so bright, and a bunch of random people discussed their view about the meaning of life and where humanity will go. The amount of wisdom shared was beyond what i can describe... and there sure is a reason, why people who went to Burning Man once, keep going every year.

The best show I saw in my live was David Bowie in a very small venue In Germany. The stars literally aligned for me to be there, and he played for 3.5hours to honor the time he lived in Germany. I was standing pretty much face to face with him. What a genius, and he certainly burned the candle from both ends... and should he have met Dr. Jack to reset his POMC pathways.

Last year i decided to let go of all the old musicians who I loved when I was younger. just because i liked them then, doesn't mean they are making my live fuller now.
