### Gagnrad — 2014-05-01T23:15:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-124428>

I just read this rebuke:

"You take it for granted that every man in the world is crooked except yourselves."​

That struck me, and I tried reminded myself not to think the worst of anyone. It made me think of Søren Kierkegaard, whom Jack mentioned recently, specifically of his essay "Love Covereth a Multitude of Sins".

“
Wherever love is present, it spreads bold confidence. We like to be near someone who loves, because he casts out fear. Whereas the mistrustful person scares everyone away, whereas the crafty and cunning spread anxiety and painful disquietude around them, whereas the presence of a domineering person is as oppressive as the heavy pressure of sultry air--love gives bold confidence. …

_Love hides a multitude of sins, because love prevents the sin from coming into existence, smothers it at birth …_

The authorities frequently have to devise very ingenious ways to keep a criminal imprisoned, and the physician often uses great inventiveness in working out coercive means to control an insane person, but in connection with sin there are no surroundings as controlling, but also there are no controlling surroundings as redeeming, as love. How often has not the anger that smouldered within, only waiting for an occasion, how often has it not been smothered because love gave no occasion! … Has not the vexation in the soul often been stilled that was so sure of and so prepared, yes, so poised for finding ever new occasions to be vexed by the world, by people, by God, by everything, has it not often been stilled by a more lenient mood, because love gave no occasion whatever to be vexed! …
”
​

Stanford:

> Søren Aabye Kierkegaard (b. 1813, d. 1855) was a profound and prolific writer in the Danish “golden age” of intellectual and artistic activity. His work crosses the boundaries of philosophy, theology, psychology, literary criticism, devotional literature and fiction. ...

[http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/kierkegaard/](http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/kierkegaard/)

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-02T09:01:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-124523>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=124425)
>
> An attractive book, I can imagine there are more gems within it's covers. I may add it to my collection

It has.

There's this translation by John Milton of one of Horace's Odes, for example. I think it captures the pain of erotic love very well. I remember someone recently commenting that you don't have to explain why roses are a symbol of love: first you see the beauty of the flower, and then you notice the thorns.

**HORACE, ODES 1. 5, TRANS. BY JOHN MILTON**
**The Fifth Ode of Horace. Lib. I**

Quis multa gracilis te puer in Rosa, _Rendred almost word for word without Rhyme according to the Latin Measure, as near as the Language will permit_

What slender Youth bedewed with liquid odours
Courts thee on Roses in some pleasant Cave,
_Pyrrha_ for whom bind’st thou
In wreaths thy golden Hair,
Plain in thy neatness? O how oft shall he
On Faith and changed Gods complain: and Seas
Rough with black winds and storms
Unwonted shall admire:
Who now enjoys thee credulous, all Gold,
Who always vacant, always amiable
Hopes thee; of flattering gales
Unmindfull. Hapless they
To whom thou untri’d seems’t fair. Me in my vowd
Picture the sacred wall declares t’ have hung
My dank and dropping weeds
To the stern God of Sea.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-03T10:52:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-124845>

How about some more poetry? This, by A. E. Housman, is for the anniversary of the Great War, which falls this year.

**Epitaph on an Army of Mercenaries**

These, in the day when heaven was falling,
The hour when earth's foundations fled,
Followed their mercenary calling,
And took their wages, and are dead.
Their shoulders held the sky suspended;
They stood, and earth's foundations stay;
What God abandoned, these defended,
And saved the sum of things for pay.​

This is the B.E.F. in 1914. The B.E.F. was a professional army, hence "Army of Mercenaries". The Kaiser referred to it as "Britain's contemptible little army".

The Kaiser seems to have been good at projecting an unattractive personality through his words. Here's what he said about the Chinese:

> “Should you encounter the enemy, he will be defeated! No quarter will be given! Prisoners will not be taken! Whoever falls into your hands is forfeited. Just as a thousand years ago the Huns under their King Attila made a name for themselves, one that even today makes them seem mighty in history and legend, may the name German be affirmed by you in such a way in China that no Chinese will ever again dare to look cross-eyed at a German.”

Anyway, small the B.E.F may have been, but the British and French stopped the German advance on the Marne. These old soldiers were so fast at loading and firing their rifles that German units are said to have believed they were advancing against machine guns when it was simply rifle fire.

Here is one of the cleverest of Kipling's short stories on the War. When you get to the end of the story you realise that it has to be read in a totally different way. (This really is a story that can "only be understood backwards".) I shan't say any more, so as not to spoil it for anyone who sees this and is interested enough to read it:

[http://www.greatwar.nl/books/gardener/gardener.html](http://www.greatwar.nl/books/gardener/gardener.html)

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-06T13:10:14+0300

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-06T13:30:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-125455>

George Borrow:

I was bidding him farewell, when he hemmed once or twice, and said, that as he did not live far off, he hoped that I would go with him and taste some of his mead. As I had never tasted mead, of which I had frequently read in the compositions of the Welsh bards, and, moreover, felt rather thirsty from the heat of the day, I told him that I should have great pleasure in attending him. ...

Having given my horse his portion, I told the old man that I was ready to taste his mead as soon as he pleased, whereupon he ushered me into his cottage, where, making me sit down by a deal table in a neatly sanded kitchen, he produced from an old-fashioned closet a bottle, holding about a quart, and a couple of cups, which might each contain about half a pint, then opening the bottle and filling the cups with a brown-coloured liquor, he handed one to me, and taking a seat opposite to me, he lifted the other, nodded, and saying to me—“Health and welcome,” placed it to his lips and drank.

“Health and thanks,” I replied; and being very thirsty, emptied my cup at a draught; I had scarcely done so, however, when I half repented. The mead was deliciously sweet and mellow, but appeared strong as brandy; my eyes reeled in my head, and my brain became slightly dizzy. “Mead is a strong drink,” said the old man, as he looked at me, with a half smile on his countenance. “This is at any rate,” said I, “so strong, indeed, that I would not drink another cup for any consideration.” “And I would not ask you,” said the old man; “for, if you did, you would most probably be stupid all day, and wake the next morning with a headache. Mead is a good drink, but woundily strong, especially to those who be not used to it, as I suppose you are not.” “Where do you get it?” said I. “I make it myself,” said the old man, “from the honey which my bees make.” “Have you many bees?” I inquired. “A great many,” said the old man. “And do you keep them,” said I, “for the sake of making mead with their honey?” “I keep them,” he replied, “partly because I am fond of them, and partly for what they bring me in; they make me a great deal of honey, some of which I sell, and with a little I make some mead to warm my poor heart with, or occasionally to treat a friend with like yourself.” “And do you support yourself entirely by means of your bees?” “No,” said the old man; “I have a little bit of ground behind my house, which is my principal means of support.” “And do you live alone?” “Yes,” said he; “with the exception of the bees and the donkey, I live quite alone.” “And have you always lived alone?” The old man emptied his cup, and his heart being warmed with the mead, he told his history, which was simplicity itself. ...​

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/422/422-h/422-h.htm](http://www.gutenberg.org/files/422/422-h/422-h.htm)

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-06T20:07:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-125522>

I found this over the bank-holiday weekend. It reminds me a little of Walter de la Mere and at the same time is wholly of the Hebrides where it was written. I think it perfect in its way. It dates from 1937 and is by Alistair Mclean.

Said the thriving man to the tinker’s wife,
“Spell me the secret of your happy life.

“I could not bide it, I was never made
To couch in bracken with the birch for shade.

“And hills I love not, their ironic smile
Seems scornful of the things I hold worth while.

“Nor am I ravished of the woodland note
That breaks in tumult from the merle’s apt throat.

“Yet you love them all. But why? What’s the art,
That turns rose-petal into quiet of heart?”

“Ah, easy to answer,” she softly said,
“I’m richer than you—that’s your riddle read.

“Have you heard fir-music? Or the whisp’ring tales
That the tide brings in when the white moon pales?

“Or glimpsed the slim grace of the tall June grass
Bowing demurely to the winds that pass?

“Or strayed through a glen where wild violet grew
And thought, “Well, Heaven will be nothing new?”

“So I envy none—if I’m poor, I’m free
To the feast love spreads for the like o’ me.”

​

Here's the famous passage on the hazelnut from Lady Julian. Whatever anyone makes of this aside the beauty, clarity and, as she might have said, homeliness of her expression can hardly be denied:

[http://www.ccel.org/ccel/julian/revelations.ii.ii.html](http://www.ccel.org/ccel/julian/revelations.ii.ii.html)

> … I marvelled how it might last, for methought it might suddenly have fallen to naught for little\[ness\]

There is kind of trust in the nature of things she has that is very appealing. She was actually living in a fairly disturbed time. But at bottom she is sure that

al shal be wel, and al shall be wel, and al manner thyng shal be well
​

Here's a thought. Maybe the Ancestral Health Movement is more appealing when it strikes a hopeful note rather than trading off fear.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-09T12:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-126007>

Here's an interview with an expert from the U.S. NIH on omega-3s and omega-6s:

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013...-more-heart-deaths-nih-scientist-joe-hibbeln/](http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2013/03/10/vegetable-oil-associated-with-more-heart-deaths-nih-scientist-joe-hibbeln/)

What's particularly interesting is that they found looking at data from a Sydney study from the 1960s that men who were told to eat omega-6 rich oils instead of butter did, indeed, achieve lower cholesterol scores. Unfortunately, they died more often. Heart disease death was higher, and all-cause mortality was also higher. This was "lost data" until last year.

### Gagnrad — 2014-05-13T17:02:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-126651>

Here's a remarkable essay from one of the greatest living philosophers in the English-speaking world on fake ideas and fake emotions. It's mostly about high culture and art in particular:

> The life of the mind has its intrinsic methods and rewards. It is concerned with the true, the beautiful and the good, which between them define the scope of reasoning and the goals of serious enquiry. But each of those goals can be faked, and one of the most interesting developments in our educational and cultural institutions over the past half century is the extent to which fake culture and fake scholarship have driven out the true varieties. It is important to ask why.

[http://aeon.co/magazine/world-views/roger-scruton-fake-culture/](http://aeon.co/magazine/world-views/roger-scruton-fake-culture/)

### sooperb — 2014-05-13T18:08:56+0300

Faking is a social activity in which people act together to draw a veil over unwanted realities and encourage each other in the exercise of their illusory powers."_

### Gagnrad — 2015-09-08T21:52:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-176790>

Oh, dear, spam.

I once posted an email address online some years ago in a discussion about Apple computers. Never again. Any email address that's online anywhere gets read by automated devices and, as it were, hoovered up to be spammed.

I just got a spam email that that reads: "There are four women within ten miles of you looking for casual sex."

Blimey! If that doesn't take the biscuit.

This is just link-bait, of course. But actually, modern mores being what they are, I should think that 4 is an underestimate for an area of that size. But never mind …

Four, huh? Perhaps when I was on the train the other day the girl in the very short white dress who stepped back and pushed her bottom into my groin was one of them. I guess it could have been an accident, but she was certainly in no hurry to remove it again, and I couldn't step back without making inappropriate contact with the woman behind me. Southern Railways -

[http://www.southernrailway.com](http://www.southernrailway.com)

\- this is _your_ fault. Please consider buying some more coaches, so that people can sit down.

### Gagnrad — 2015-09-08T22:13:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-176791>

Here's a poem that's relevant to the season - "Spring and Fall" by Gerard Manley Hopkins:

[http://www.potw.org/archive/potw29.html](http://www.potw.org/archive/potw29.html)

Those who aren't familiar with it may like to take a look at the notes on the poem here:

[http://www.sparknotes.com/poetry/hopkins/section4.rhtml](http://www.sparknotes.com/poetry/hopkins/section4.rhtml)

It's a shame they've misquoted the ninth line there. There are some useful comments there, though. I like the way that the rhythm and the changes within it are pointed to, and that the alliteration is mentioned. The writer is mistaken in saying "Hopkins’s choice of the American word 'fall' rather than the British 'autumn' is deliberate" though. "Fall" is old usage over here, although it was long "dialectical" and is now obsolete.

What I think is nice is that it should become obvious upon reflection upon what's said there that the form and content are inseparable. Can that point be made too often? The poem is as it is because of this rhythm - and because it is _just these words in just this order_. (And this, of course, is why poetry is unlike certain other forms of discourse - the reader must have the realisation I'm pointing to for himself here - as the naive have not yet realised.)

It would, of course, be a mistake to go away from those notes having the notion that they held the "meaning" of the poem. They're useful comment upon the poem: they don't _replace_ it. Works of art don't state their conclusions (insofar as they have them); they _show_ them.

And criticism is always no more than a provisional statement that forms the starting point for further discussion. So, for example, I could question whether this poem is really about "mortality" and not rather about "loss". And there might be an interesting discussion there. But in the end, the point is not what is said about the poem, but the poem itself, and that is what we must return to:

[http://www.potw.org/archive/potw29.html](http://www.potw.org/archive/potw29.html)

### Gagnrad — 2015-09-24T08:51:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-177715>

I was catching up on a few podcasts - don't bother with them much now. Anyway, one was Robb Wolf's podcast from a few weeks back, and he was speaking to Dr. Kirk Parsley the ex-U.S. Navy doctor. This was actually quite interesting. It turns out that Kirk Parsley, over many years, developed a product to help Navy special forces soldiers sleep. It is a huge problem for many of them, because of the demands of their job. There are ingredients in it that one might expect, such as D3 and magnesium, but actually a host of others as well. I didn't look into the information on Doc Parsley's site, because it's very extensive, but if anyone's interested here it is:

[http://www.sleepcocktails.com](http://www.sleepcocktails.com)

He told Robb they're going to have to change the site name, because many companies and organisations block access to the site, since it would seem to be alcohol-related.

This shows how the year is moving on: I recently bought my Christmas cards. They come from a local hospital, which has just had a batch printed for sale. Below is a shot I took in grounds of that hospital some while ago. It's a vey beautiful and peaceful place with really extensive grounds tucked into what is still a fairly secluded area of the Sussex–Kent border.

### Gagnrad — 2015-09-28T19:56:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-178035>

Here's an interesting interview from Vinnie Tortorich, who bills himself as America's Angriest Trainer. Here he's talking to a doctor about pain.

What's quite interesting is that this doctor says that chronic pain - as opposed to acute pain - is often not "structural". He says we need to look at "the psyche, the emotions, the patient's thinking patterns". He's saying that these feed into the nervous system and often make the pain far worse or even keep it alive when, otherwise, it might just go away. This is all very interesting and, it seems to me, plausible. It certainly fits in with several observations and experiences that we know about from elsewhere. And he also says that by using various techniques to challenge and break down some of these thinking patterns he can and has made some people's chronic pain go away.

Here's the interview on Vinnie's site:

[http://vinnietortorich.com/2015/09/fighting-pain-with-the-brain-with-dr-schecter/](http://vinnietortorich.com/2015/09/fighting-pain-with-the-brain-with-dr-schecter/)

This interview can also be found in iTunes.

[https://itunes.apple.com/podcast/celebrity-fitness-trainer/id521811037?mt=2](https://itunes.apple.com/podcast/celebrity-fitness-trainer/id521811037?mt=2)

### Gagnrad — 2015-09-29T20:45:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-178161>

> [b612 said:](/goto/post?id=178160)
>
> Why do you care things are paleo or not.

Do I? I'm actually more WAPF than paleo.

But this site's owner said that Cordain's _The Paleo Answer_ was so spot on as regarded diet that he didn't need to do any research himself on the matter. (I forget where he said this: but he did say it.) He did add, however, that Cordain had not seen all the implications of his own data, but - hey - he wasn't exactly running the guy down.

I doubt anyone with sense would myself. There's some 3rd-rate knockdown stuff about paleo (and low carb) around on the internet now. Some of it is motivated by malice - people don't like to be told they're wrong (even when they are). Some of it is pure ignorance. I think that battle was won _intellectually_ a long time ago, though. Cordain is a little rigid and I doubt a few lentils, for example, or the occasional slice of sourdough bread is going to harm most people. But he's made a very useful contribution to the debate. This isn't just anyone slinging around a few simple ideas you know (even if undereducated loudmouthed fools like Alan Levinovitz might think so). Cordain is a qualified biochemist and spends hours each day reading academic papers and thinking about these matters. _The Paleo Answer_ is the fruit of some quarter of a century of that. As I say, I prefer the WAPF approach, but I respect Cordain. I don't throw stones at better men than I.

### Gagnrad — 2015-10-06T16:55:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-178710>

A friend tells me that berberine is the anti-antibiotic. What I think this probably means is that she's seen some data that suggest that it helps beneficial gut bacteria to flourish (presumably by killing less helpful ones, as some herbs will). Antibiotics can, of course, be useful, but they do tend to kill off what would have been better left alone along with what they're aimed at. And in this way they make space for overgrowths that we could do without. So anything that would, or might in some cases, reverse that effect would obviously be a good thing. I know that berberine has long been touted as useful for stabilising bloodsugar (and I think it has an ancient history in this role). I don't know what its mechanism of action is thought to be, but perhaps the improvements in insulin sensitivity it brings might be mediated by an effect on the microbiome.

Floramend looks like an interesting probiotic:

[http://uk.iherb.com/Thorne-Research...e-Caps/41208?gclid=CK-8gPOAq8gCFeOD2wodR0AJ9w](http://uk.iherb.com/Thorne-Research-FloraMend-Prime-Probiotic-30-Veggie-Caps/41208?gclid=CK-8gPOAq8gCFeOD2wodR0AJ9w)
This one has less strains of bacteria than some (compare Bio-Kult recommended by the GAPS people and originally developed in consultation with Dr. Natasha Campbell-McBride) but is high-potency. Floramend has three strains in it, including the vitally important _Bifidobacterium longum_, which, I believe, one should get at birth and keep, but which some of us are careless enough to lose. (Spot the reference to Mr. Worthing there. And come to think of it, the capsules could be kept in a handbag. "A handbag!") The coating on these capsules is also supposed to be proof against stomach acid, so the capsule doesn't open too soon. But then some probiotics are supplied as powders and seem to work. The Floramend does not have to be kept refrigerated.
It seems likely that a healthy microbiome is highly diverse. (This isn't certain, and people have argued otherwise, but that's the most common opinion currently, and the greater microbial diversity found in populations that have not been exposed to antibiotics, and other disrupters, supports that view.) It's also thought that, as with ecosystems, certain species are keystone species. Some do appear to have very special roles, and some while not numerous are associated with a good state of health. (Compare how very small numbers of a predator introduced into an ecosystem can improve its overall functioning by keeping a prey animal that's, for example, overgrazing withing bounds, or how a few wolves can stop a coyote population from exploding.) One example might be _Akkermansia municiphila_. That seems to have a symbiotic role: it feeds on mucus and, as it were, in exchange helps to keep down opportunistic pathogens. The Ireland rugby team had their microbiomes sequenced a while ago. They tended to have higher levels than the general population of some of these important symbionts, such as Akkermansia. Akkermansia has been found to be very low or absent in samples of some populations, such as the obese and diabetics. Akkermansia, by the way, can be helped by eating some specific foods, such as pomegranate seeds, but you have to have a viable population there to be helped in the first place.

### Gagnrad — 2015-10-06T16:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-178711>

Perhaps I should have waited for some snow before posting this one.

Robert Frost
**Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening**

Whose woods these are I think I know.
His house is in the village though;
He will not see me stopping here
To watch his woods fill up with snow.
My little horse must think it queer
To stop without a farmhouse near
Between the woods and frozen lake
The darkest evening of the year.
He gives his harness bells a shake
To ask if there is some mistake.
The only other sound’s the sweep
Of easy wind and downy flake.
The woods are lovely, dark and deep,
But I have promises to keep,
And miles to go before I sleep,
And miles to go before I sleep.

My thought, too. I think as problems arise people find ways to deal with them. C.f. Dentists. No one needed them till they needed them, but then they did ... Weston Price is very good on this.

> Gosh, Drummond's end was sure dramatic, similar to that family killed in France a couple of years ago by some unknown assassin. His book is a tad expensive but I'd bet it's an interesting read, for those with enough knowledge to follow it if it's very academic.

No, it's written for a general audience. Tim Noakes is a big fan of Drummond, BTW. The interesting book is the book about the murder, though. _Everyone_ should read that. Go on: get a copy. It's only a penny secondhand. What's to lose?

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Vitamin-Murders-Killed-Healthy-Britain/dp/1846270146/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Vitamin-Murders-Killed-Healthy-Britain/dp/1846270146/)

The question is _who_ carried the murder out. The public answer is "crazy backward French peasant". The "conspiracy" answer is "secret service assassination". AFAICT, Professor Michael Crawford thinks the latter. Tim Noakes gives the impression he does, too. I don't know. Maybe. There is stuff that points that way. Drummond was definitely working for British Intelligence in _some_ capacity. And there are witness statements regarding people asking about his movements and evidently following him. There's also a tale of some curiously "missing" documents connected with the case.

And this is all tied up with the explosion in chemical agriculture after the war and with a French Plant that was doing that stuff. Furthermore, some of these chemicals are a bit double-use: they started life as chemical weapons and were later re-purposed as pesticides. (Straight up. Robb Wolf recently commented on this in a podcast.) So, yeah, intelligence agencies _could_ have been interested in what Drummond was up to.

Maybe the Soviets rubbed him out. Or maybe he annoyed British - or French - Intelligence in some way. But maybe it really was just a French peasant. Who could tell at this distance in time?

> In the north west our water is not fluoridised thank goodness. They are very heavy handed with chlorine though, you can smell it when you turn on the tap. I've used water filtration for nearly 30 years, I wish we had a well!

Same here. Ozone would be better, but AFAIK companies that use that generally use chlorine as well and the combination causes further problems. Oh, dear ...

> "At this point, what can we do?" I can only suggest wild camping, in a warm country by an unpolluted lake or river, fancy trying it ?

Well, golly, I hope you didn't mean the "we" in one sense it could have: I think I'd better leave married women alone.

> In 1983 award-winning physicist Gary Holz was diagnosed with chronic progressive multiple sclerosis. By 1988 he was a quadriplegic. Then, in 1994, his doctors told him he had two years to live. Arriving in a wheelchair, alone, with almost no feeling left from the neck down, Holz embarked on a remarkable healing transformation of body, mind, and spirit and discovered his own gift for healing others. Written at the request of the Aboriginal healers Holz worked with, this book reveals the beliefs and principles of the 60,000-year-old healing system of the Aborigines of Australia, the world's oldest continuous culture. ...

### Gagnrad — 2015-10-15T19:27:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-179585>

We now have posters for 23andme in British trains.

Hmmmm …

I wonder. I guess it's cool if you've got the cash, but just how useful is it?

There are worries around (a) privacy and (b) supposed "genetic susceptibility" to certain conditions (but, note, does this even matter if the relevant genes aren't "switched on"? (Yes, I know it's more complicated than that).)

So, anyway, my impression is that they're now selling on "human interest" takeaways - like "how Irish or French or German are you?" … or - the ultimate tease - how _Neanderthaler_ are you?

IMO, this is OK as far as it goes, but nothing is as clear cut as 23andme would like you to think.

Current political boundaries aren't where they've always been. See, for example:

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dál\_Riata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dál_Riata)

(And, in this context, remember that communication by sea was once far easier than travel over rutted and boggy roads.)

If you look at the broad picture behind this - try, for example, Stephen Oppemheimer's work - what you find is this. There were two "Ice Age Refuges". These were places where people retreated when things just got too cold. One was in what is now Spain; the other, in what is now the Ukraine. As the Ice Sheet retreated people moved out from these places again. Everyone with European ancestry is descended from these people in one way or another - there's no other way to cut it - and really re-imagining people as having descent from particular places that map onto current nations (which are, at the end of the day, political constructs) is a sleight of hand. It's _interesting_ to people - and a come-on to fork over the money - but … well, take it with a pinch of salt. And, anyway, gene pools, as I hope most people are aware, are "open".

So you can look at some particular gene - say the Y-chromosome - and, sure, there will be _some_ variants on that that will be late arrivals and that have a geographically limited distribution that allows you to map that variation back to its likely origin. But, well, not everyone in a given modern national population will have that variant - perhaps a minority will - so what's the interest? AFAICT, the _real_ interest is that if it turns up elsewhere you know that there was a movement from A to B. Maybe only one in ten Norse had this allele. But if it turns up in one of 30 inhabitants of, say, Cumberland and Westmoreland, then that tells you that there was a historic movement of population from Norway to the NW coast of England - which should surprise no-one given what we already know from the historical records, linguistic analysis of dialects, and so on.

I think what the average person does not realise is that they never see the _raw data_ from genetic analysis. And they simply believe whatever they're told about it. What they're told isn't totally fantasy, of course, but there's a whole raft of assumptions and statistical jiggery-pokery that the average person is not even aware of.

I think the same goes, to some extent, for uBiome or American/British Gut data. It doesn't tell you what's _there_ in some uncomplicated and error-free manner. It gives you an interesting snapshot subject to sampling and processing errors.

# Thread: Washer/Drier near the bedroom...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/>

### Hope — 2014-01-20T22:03:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/post-106323>

any of you have a W/D near the bedroom? We are looking at houses and one has this thing. I guess we can turn off breakers when not in use, but is that enough?

Anyone of you have this in their house and if so, what do you do?

### caroline — 2014-01-20T23:12:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/post-106353>

It should be okay if you can turn it off at the breaker...... that is what we do for our bedrooms, microwaves etc......

The tough ones are refrigerators and stoves. I remember Nick saying that there was an old fridge on the other sides of his son's bedroom wall and it had very high EMF. His son was very ill .....

### Hope — 2014-01-21T00:12:13+0200

Ugh. That's what sucks with townhouses...which is what we are mostly looking at. Thankfully in our case, mostly it's bedrooms meeting bedrooms on that level. though there could be a TV on the other side, which means I gotta test.

Thanks Audrey!

I will most definitely talk to M Neuert again, too, what to do if we get stuck. I know those things are yucky. but after starting to read Body Electric, I wanna reduce stuff even more. As much as I can.

### caroline — 2014-01-21T02:51:05+0200

Hope - you are a woman on a mission......

### cinnamon — 2014-01-21T02:54:19+0200

### caroline — 2014-01-21T02:58:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/post-106387>

It truly, truly is....... Hope - you need to start a commune somewhere ...... we need to check out Martin's homestead!

We need a bunch of Yurts - and we could travel around..... How much property does JK have ???

we could weed his garden or something .... or clean the chicken coop.....

### Gracelind — 2014-01-21T07:59:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/post-106429>

Another issue with washer/dryer near bedrooms might be ventilation and the possibility of mold and dust/lint in the air.

### Hope — 2014-01-21T23:03:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/washer-drier-near-the-bedroom.9401/post-106506>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=106429)
>
> Another issue with washer/dryer near bedrooms might be ventilation and the possibility of mold and dust/lint in the air.

Yep, I know. It's what helped cause mold in our current house. No good. I did send out for a HERTSMI2 on this possible rental.

Audrey, yes I am on a mission. I am certain people would think I am crazy. I FINALLY started using Michael's body voltage meter and wow. I should sleep in the kitchen or living room. Not the bedroom. And not near the windows. It is so weird; something must be going on in the wiring in the wall. Going to bring it with me to any new house we see. Though the realtor is already getting fed up after going out with us twice (we are renting, still). But I don't care. I have lost WAY too much of my health for mold and this wifi hell.

Just so thankful for all I'm learning here....no matter what.

And yes, my dream is to live in the middle of nowhere. I wish I could convince DH of that. But, I have actually thought of renting a small place in the mountains somewhere, on a temporary basis, to go heal, just see DH on weekends for some time. I feel SO different there. And then today with those body voltage meter readings...whoa. Anyway I digress.

But I do agree with the commune. If I were wealthy, I'd start a healing community for people to come. No wifi, as little electricity as possible, magneticos in all the beds. Though I have to research what Dr K said last night....so hope I can hear the recap. Gotta listen again to what he said to do if you're dehydrated...redox potential shot. Can't wait. I am dehydrated still I am sure.

### ghina — 2014-01-22T01:35:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ghinas-journal.9407/post-106542>

Thank you Caroline and nicd.

I've tracking my labs since 2010.
\* My B/C ratio over time bounce around a a bit but they are (rounded): 15, 20, 23, 18, 22.

\* What's really interesting is that my last quarter of lab tests (before the magnetico pad) showed a 50 pt drop in my cholesterol; my VAP scores also went down.

\* My glucose went down by 30pts (though my A1C stayed the same and is in an upward trend continuously). My insulin is normal.

\* My 4 point saliva cortisol (ASI) is above reference range (scores are 4 times the top of the reference range) except for the afternoon which dips low enough to be in the morning reference range!!

\* I do have low SIGA, but my gliadin is ok.

\* My hormones (progesterone, estrogen, thyroid (free and reverse and T3 variations) are all low and I'm supplementing by prescription (and are why I get my labs every quarter)

\* Extremely low RBC magnesium that doesn't rise even with shots, but am thinking of his recommendation of threnoate in the morning, orotate in the afternoon, malate in the evening.

\* In terms of mitochondria and the liver:.
\+ ALT is always below 4 (Reference Range 6-40)
\+ The other ones found on the test are normal

\* Esonophils, Neutrophils etc are all normal ranges

So in summary I'm thinking my mitochondria are shot, and I need to read up on them. I already take CoQ10 and PQQ, but I may need to deep dive to address. My hormones are currently being monitored so though they need to be fixed, no one is checking out the mitochondria with focus. Yes that would explain by 18+ years of chronic fatigue.

### caroline — 2014-01-22T03:02:20+0200

Ghina - you have to start very slowly. Dr. K. is very deliberate about what he tells us to do and how - make no mistake about that!

Do you have a gold membership? you would have access to all the webinars and follow up live Q&A. You can listen as many times as you want and whenever you want. Think of it as an investment in your health/life/journey to optimal. We need to get this right. Where else would you get this opportunity?

Don't forget to listen to your gut feelings too .... Dr.K. often says the best Doctor is the one in your own head!

C/T turns on your internal heater - there are huge success stories here.

go back and read the protocol again to make sure you have all the details correct. Just start with face dunks - no time line ... just do what you can do.

Don't ever hesitate to ask questions if you don't understand something ... we are all in the same boat - I promise you.

CT helps reset the internal thermostat, trust me. I was a FREEZE baby with a thyroid problem. I wore sweatshirts in summer and put my space heater on at work when it was 90 outside. I started CT last summer in order to get a little colder before bed (we do not have central air) and the internal thermostat changed. Now, I can sit in a 40 F bath for 90 minutes in the winter. I have only wore a jacket 5 days this winter and that was when the temps were -30 to -50.

My husband thinks I am nuts but he does see that I am no longer cold all the time and that the sex drive has returned like never before so he understands. He won't take one and it awesome about letting me warm up with him. A lot of times after CT I can feel an electron transfer when we cuddle or just touch.

I started off slow by sitting in the bath and letting the water fill up. Now, I just jump in and suck it up for the first minute till I get used to it.

I agree with caroline, read CT#7, it is LOADED with key stuff.

Nicole

### caroline — 2014-01-22T07:18:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ghinas-journal.9407/post-106591>

Honestly Ghina - just do what you can do. I freeze large baggies of ice in my freezer and then put on a compression shirt and lay down with a bag on my tummy. I then put it right on my skin .... but you don't have to do that. I never had a problem with that but you might.

It is baby steps - don't forget this is YOUR journey - not someone else's.

My water comes out of the tap at 75 degrees - there is no way I can get it cold enough- so I just get in with a compression shirt on and puts three bags of ice on my torso. easy...

When you are face dunking - it is great for the skin and complexion - you will look rosy and health and you won't need a face lift down the track!! win, win, win!

Do walk around with less clothing and go to bed naked ... it is great!

The whole point is it is your journey - you have issues that I don't have or Dr. Kruse doesn't have.

You just have to be aware that C/T reduces inflammation ... it is primordial. Start slow and before you know it you will crave the cold! honest injun!

keep reading and asking questions. I once asked Dr. K. what I said was a really dopey question ... he said it wasn't a dopey question ... the dopey thing was I had waited so long to ask!

He loves questions, he loves to know you are trying to figure it out, he knows a lot of us aren't science geeks ..... and he also has said - he doesn't know what we don't know unless we ask questions.

this is his mission to teach us to learn all of this on some level and to help our docs to be better and pay it forward.

I was buying a dress yesterday and the young woman asked me what I was doing - before long there was a bunch of people standing around and talking about paleo etc.

### ghina — 2014-02-20T17:22:25+0200

I've been doing CT now for a little while. My face dunks are feeling less cold. And I can do some bath CT by getting partially in a warm tub, and using ice packs on the torso. I just don't think it should be this painful in my hands and feet with elevated sugar readings.

### ghina — 2014-03-07T03:54:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ghinas-journal.9407/post-113899>

Hi Caroline,

I'm in an older 1955 brick house that is in the middle of a corner lot, which means that I have above ground power lines. The corner of my bedroom is roughly 50 ft from the corner on the street.

Not using grounding sheets, instead I've purchased a grounding cord that plugs into my grounded surge protector and a USB port on my computer. I've also bought a Harapad and an antistatic mat for the computer. I'm not doing anything in particular in my bedroom, but the Magnetico was my move to help with the EMFs in the first place. I only got the lowest gauss pad. And I'm afraid to try a higher gauss pad.

I don't have and had never had cell phones/tvs in my room. The only thing plugged in my clock radio, but it's on the other side of the room and is angled so i have to get up to find the the right angle to read it.

I tried to turn off the wifi in the evening before removing the magnetico and test to see if my symptoms were better and they did not have a noticeable effect. I did call and speak with Dr. Bonlie, I think I was a puzzle to him too as he recommended I speak to a doctor in Baltimore, but that is impractical for me. I listened to two of his podcasts from his site and it makes me very excited, but then very sad. I also bought Pluggz shoes for when I'm out, but that is new and wouldn't have had the initial weird effect. I've also installed FLux on my computer and cell phone.

I have noticed since aerating the water that I need to urinate very soon after drinking the water. I like to interpret that as structured water is replacing bulk water, but I'm not sure. I'm also drinking even when I'm not thirsty. But since I've removed the pad, the neuropathy has really lessened. I'm waking up feeling better, though blood sugar has not yet returned to normal yet.

So I feel like I'm addressing the 3 big things:

1.  Water (aeration, halides removed)
2.  Photoelectric (blocking blue light, resetting my circadian clock using light)
3.  EMF (grounding shoes, grounding cord, laptop shields, ~magnetico~)

Now I have a picture up, so maybe this will be interesting to Jack?!

He mentioned taking DMSA just before sleeping on the pad to mop up released mercury. I had already chelated about 100 times (some with DMSA) and was pretty sure there would only be a tiny amount left if any, so it didn't occur to me that this dynamic might be in play. Nonetheless I had some DMSA in my cupboard of thousands of pills, so I tried it. Took some DMSA and slept on the magnetico. Guess what? No tingling or prickliness. I began to suspect that the neuropathy could be from released mercury that I was not removing from my body. My glutathione stores are well below average, and I'm dehydrated. I still have high blood sugar (165), but it's lower than it used to be. I'm going to experiment with the DMSA.

Interestingly I noticed that the sulfur smell was being eliminated from both "number 1" and "number 2". I remember Dr. Tim stating that when your gut is overwhelmed and cannot eliminate that it shunts detox to the kidneys. This could explain the dehydration.

My current stance is do what I was already doing (CT, EpiPaleo, EMF, Water etc) plus: 1) heal the gut, which could put less stress on the kidneys and improve the BUN/Creatinine ratio, which would indicate improved redox. 2)Use DMSA and Magnetico at various amounts to see if there is a correlation over time. 3) continue to monitor blood sugar. ### sooperb — 2014-03-17T16:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ghinas-journal.9407/post-115938>

My interpretation of what Dr. Jack meant is that people will buy into a belief, a diet or a dogma but you can't gain the benefits if you don't follow the prescription. So, for example, Leptin reset is a strict protocol, eat within 30 mins of rising, big breakfast etc. etc. You can't just follow part of it and expect it to work, you have to follow the instructions to the letter. If you don't, you are fooling yourself into believing that you are doing it and kind of forgetting the bits that don't appeal or substituting your own idea of one of the parts, maybe eating all veggies for breakfast but with lots of fat. When you don't get the results you expected, you blame the prescription and not your lack of adherence. I hope that makes sense even if it a bit long-winded lol.

# Thread: Is it what you eat or what you don't eat?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-21T09:20:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/post-106433>

I think it's both. Not eating poisonous plants or cadmium-concentrating polar bear liver, not to mention more everyday fare like gluten-containing grains and HFCS, is obviously necessary. And I think high quality, naturally raised food is the best thing to eat.

But I was thinking specifically of a remark Mike Eades made about terry Wahls' remarkable recovery. Now, she recommends, IIRC, two cups of cruciferous vegetables, two cups of red and orange vegetables, and two cups of sulphur-containg vegetables a day. Mike Eades gave her TED talk a very favourable review. Afterwards, someone popped up in his comments and said, what many people must have been thinking, that's an awful lot of vegetables to chew your way through. Dr. Eades replied with something along the lines of: Yes, it is; but is it what she's eating that counts or what she's not eating?

I thought of this later when I found that the ex-Polish Army doctor Jan Kwasnieski had claimed some success with treating multiple sclerosis with a low-carb diet. Nothing special in the way of seeking out antioxidants and plant phyto-nutrients or anything, mostly just prescribing a diet that, if memory serves, works out to about 80% fat, 13% protein, 7% carbohydrate. Now if there are any academic journal articles on this I guess they'll be in Polish and most of us couldn't read 'em. Interesting, though.

I thought of it again when reading David Perlmutter's new book _Grain Brain_. Perlmutter says that taking antioxidants doesn't actually get you very far and that the point is to switch on your own body's antioxidants. Maybe this is relevant.

I'm not knocking deeply-coloured vegetables, etc. and I do eat them -- though not in enormous quantities. But maybe it's the getting gluten and excessive carbohydrate out your diet that makes most of the difference. And I'm inclined to think there'd be little or no point in, say, swallowing vitamin C capsules if you're still, as it were, throwing paraffin on the fire.

It makes me think about those Nutrient Density tables again, too -- not for the first time. So what if you're "over the line" for this or that if your diet is basically unbalanced? Just "enough" C from all that fruit juice but eleven servings of grains a day on top (thank you USDA!). And this is beside the problem Jack has often pointed out that those tables ignore the special importance of brain-specific nutrients.

### caroline — 2014-01-21T09:53:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/post-106437>

Interesting thoughts .... that is a ton of veggies!

### Da-mo — 2014-01-21T10:40:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-what-you-eat-or-what-you-dont-eat.9408/post-106444>

\>..and dont forget "WHEN you eat". JK has given that at least as much importance.

Then today I was reading EE3 and 4 while trying to help a lady with AI, and find that eating clean would help but not fix the cause of the problem, which is EMF and its affects on Calcium and Magnesium. In this case, if I understand correctly, even trying to correct the Ca and Mg through the diet would not help as it would not be able to be utilised until the cause was removed.

### SkylerTrevino — 2014-01-21T12:43:01+0200

Im 24 years old. i weigh 130 pounds im 5'10. i think im underweight for my size. So maybe i should look at the leptin rx. i plan on seeing a doctor in march as thats when my insurance kicks in. ill change my work schedule at ctc from 930pm to 6am to 6am to 230pm. try and see a gastro doc and get some labs done to see what i need to supplement add to my diet. i have psoriasis. ive had it on and off since i was a toddler. when i was younger i used to eat tomatoes whole maybe that had something to do with it. but i was rx eucerin and it went away. until i got about to 13 14 years old. got put on steroids. stop taking them at prob 15 and just used eucerin. then enbrel came along. took that for 3 months it made me depressed and more susceptible to infection and getting sick. got off of that and started looking for alternatives. slippery elm bark and another herb i cant remember but its what edgar cayce recommended. stayed away from nightshades drunk alot of water oh i also took detox tea by yogi i think it was. it cleared up pretty well. then i stopped doing that. around 17. was good until about age 20. so i just tried to stay as hydrated as possible and use coconut oil instead of petro filled lotions. and it flares up off and on. not as bad as used to be now that i use shea butter coco butter coconut oil and vitamin e oil togther.i use advanced d by purity products about 5000 units it has vitamin k (menaquinone) 80mcg, magnesium 125mg, zinc 10mg, l taurine +, quercetin+, boron+. also take natural calm magnesium.350mg. solarray zinc and copper iodine53mcg, zinc 50mg, copper 2mg. super selenium complex by life extension. 200mcg. cod liver oil by green pastures 1 table spoon. the plan is to get all my labs done. see what the docs suggest i do to fix my leaky gut while following JK protocal. after that im changing jobs. look to get my bartending license or go to australia to be a sheep herder and travel aswell we shall see. i run into alot of emf at work obviously i also have wifi on at my house. but i turn it off while im sleeping. i notice that when my phone is on wifi gets picked up from my neighbors so know im thinking i should switch rooms. i also turn my phone off when i dont need it.

### caroline — 2014-01-22T00:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skyler-trevino-journal.9411/post-106530>

Hey Skyler! I am so excited for you to go travelling and have the most monumental time.

You know you can make a lot of money in restaurants/bars if you are a good worker and personable! if you are traveling you often get paid in cash.

I have spent a fair bit of time in central Asia the last few years and I can't begin to express how travel feeds your soul and inspires your imagination. In these countries ... people have so little and yet ... they have everything and are very willing to share.

I have been lucky enuf to travel a lot during my lifetime - but the tourist stuff doesn't do it for me any more ... I need real people and real life ... and the simple things like sitting on the ground and having a picnic with people you barely know.

# Thread: Veterinary help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Linz — 2014-01-21T14:37:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106461>

I would welcome suggestions that might be worth exploring to help a young horse just diagnosed with one-sided temperohyoid osteoarthritis - a degenerative arthritic condition affecting the small bones that are part of vestibular system in head. Recommended treatment is to remove a small section of bone to prevent movement in the affected joint so that small bones don't fracture if they fuse which would lead to severe neuro damage. This does not seem to address the ongoing inflammation or bone damage but seems inevitable to prevent the severe consequence of continued movement.

The horse lives outside, unrugged, on a high fibre clean diet supplemented with seaweed, brewer's yeast, linseed, turmeric (and I can't really blame the mobile phone!).

I think something like [www.ice](http://www.ice) bonnet.com looks as though it might be a start. According to their website the horse is one of only two animals that don't have an internal system to cool blood going to the brain ( i haven't verified this anywhere else). Just wondering if this could be a reason to make it difficult to stop inflammation in the head without external help.

Homeopathic treatment over several months before diagnosis was not sufficient although I think there were some short term benefits, and Scenar treatment has helped another horse here with a fracture.

Have I missed any promising avenues to explore? Does anyone here have any knowledge of Boswellia as anti -inflammatory? It is being added to an increasing number of equine supplements now.

### SeaHorse — 2014-01-21T21:14:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106495>

You might want to look to see if somebody in your area does low photon light therapy, there are some "hats" made by Bioscan that work on the head area....just a thought. Serrapeptase is another possibility to help with damage from osteoarthritis. Precision Joint Solution is a supplement made in the US that a lot of people are having good experiences with....it has serrapeptase, HA, eggshell membrane and a few other things that you might be able to get individually...it is very pricey. Good luck.

### Linz — 2014-01-23T14:16:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106746>

Thank you SeaHorse,

The low photon light looks promising and is available here for a reasonable cost. I'm trying to get to grips with various supplements but do have to be careful with the cost. Lots of trouble with internet connnection here after high winds- just when I really need it.

I have delayed her surgery for a month to give me some time to try and reduce the inflammation first and I'm going to try to find out a bit more about mito support - mito research in horses seems to be very limited.

### HoneyChild — 2014-01-23T15:00:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106749>

I'm not 100% but I think Lynn Razaitis who runs the Iodine Workshop on Face Book takes borax for her arthritis and I think she's got a horse because her avatar is of a horse. I did a very quick search of the group and found this comment from her:-

"Our neighbors lab, who lived at our house half the time, started developing all kinds of hip problems and was miserable. I started giving her a secret morning potion of a little borax, liver powder, cod liver oil, collagen and raw egg. She perked up fast and was jumping around like a puppy. Neighbors were real excited to find something that worked."

### SeaHorse — 2014-01-23T15:09:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-106751>

From what I've read about eggshell membrane it is loaded with collagen and all the components of a healthy joint, so it makes sense.

### Linz — 2014-01-25T14:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/veterinary-help.9412/post-107013>

Thank you both. I'm looking for inspiration to find the cause at the moment.
May go the route of remedial dentistry along with cranio sacral treatment

# Thread: Starting my Leptin Reset diet!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/starting-my-leptin-reset-diet.9415/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mariaelaine — 2014-01-21T18:10:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/starting-my-leptin-reset-diet.9415/post-106476>

Yesterday I turned 33! Maybe it's because my birthday is in January, or maybe it's just my perspective on birthdays, but I've always treated my birthday as my personal New Year holiday. On my birthday for the last three years, I've made the same resolution: to try to be the healthiest me possible.

I'm a moderately athletic person who enjoys boxing (only bags, not people), various aerobic activities and over the last couple of months have begun jogging. I eat pretty cleanly as well. Clean and humane meats, eggs, veggies, whole grains with the occasional dining out cheat. Very little alcohol, no smoking or recreational drugs and no junk/processed foods. The way I eat now is very different than they way I was raised and has taken years of learning and developing good habits, learning to cook and some seriously creative financing as I'm on a very low-income budget.

My good habits (and good genes based on my parents) have given me a generally healthy disposition. As far as the basics of blood pressure, cholesterol, immune response, digestive system, I'm pretty health and always have been. What drives me towards a change is that despite a baseline of general health and an active lifestyle, I've always been overweight. Since adolescence I've carried more body fat than I would seem to need. I also have a good deal of muscle mass, I'm long-limbed, and of a medium build to begin with, but still carry a lot of weight that I'm positive I don't need.

As I get further from 30, I realize that I'll only continue to put on the pounds of fat unless I find a better balance of nutrition that works for me. That's why I'm starting a Leptin Reset diet using the Primal Blueprint Diet as a guideline. It's day two and I'm going to keep a journal using this forum a couple of days a week to help chart my experience and stay focused. I also look forward to reading others' experiences on the forum.

I'm logging my food in the Lose It app for iPhone to keep track of the protein, fat, and carb counts for the day.

Here's to 33!

### caroline — 2014-01-22T00:18:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/starting-my-leptin-reset-diet.9415/post-106521>

Happy belated Birthday Maria! and welcome. There is a journal section - if you cant find it please let me know.

Did you join the free 90 day reset? It would be perfect for you. There is a yellow banner at the top of the home page to register. Dr. Kruse just did the first webinar yesterday - if you join now I think you can still hear it. Please make that a priority - it was a great webinar on epi pale etc, .... please don't miss it.

If you ever need help - just holler!

### mamadell — 2014-01-21T19:32:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-and-relationship-between-cholesterol-levels-hormones-and-sex-hormone-binding.9419/post-106481>

new to this forum and to the paleo diet and way of life
just had a full lab report done and can't seem to find a doc in my area that can support me in coming into balance while addressing the cause and not the symptoms
i am 51 , in good shape, 118 lbs and am going thru menopause ...cycles are irregular
the lab values that concern me, are as follows :

total cholesterol 331
LDL cholesterol 239
HDL cholesterol 72
triglycerides 64
non HDL chol. ( LDL + VLDL ) 259
apoB100-calc 157
LDL-R ( real) 202
IDL cholesterol 28
Remnant Lipo ( IDL + VLDL3 ) 40

good thing is that my LDL are large buoyant

Hormone panel :
serum testosterone 27
free testosterone <0.2
progesterone 1.8
estradiol 222
dhea 196
total estrogens 391 HIGH
sex hormone binding globulin 162 HIGH

Thyroid panel :
TSH 1.21
T3 free serum 2.5
T4 free direct 1.51
reverse T3 16.1

Other values of interest :
glucose, serum 81
cortisol 10
prolactin 15.1
IGF-1 167
VIT.D 57.3
C-reactive Protein 0.11

i have had high cholesterol levels for 20 years and have noticed that the total levels are going up as i get older.
since entering menopause, my hormone levels go in and out of balance but the numbers on my sex hormone binding globulin keep going up ?
my i guess my liver is producing excess cholesterol and SHBG but why ?
currently i am following a paleo diet but before that i was mostly vegan
any insight would be greatly appreciated
thank you

SHBG - indicates how well you're clearing estrogens. looking at your estrogen/estridol you're not clearing estrogen properly. you probably also have some methylation issues.

>
> i have had high cholesterol levels for 20 years and have noticed that the total levels are going up as i get older.
> since entering menopause, my hormone levels go in and out of balance but the numbers on my sex hormone binding globulin keep going up ?
> my i guess my liver is producing excess cholesterol and SHBG but why ?
> currently i am following a paleo diet but before that i was mostly vegan
> any insight would be greatly appreciated
> thank you

am going thru menopause ...cycles are irregular
progesterone 1.8-----------may be low or ok, depends------------when was blood drawn in relation to day #1 of your cycle, how long was that cycle?
estradiol 222 ------------------------high
dhea 196 --------------------------low
total estrogens 391 -----------------high
sex hormone binding globulin 162 HIGH
cortisol 10---------------------------low (what time was blood drawn)
currently i am following a paleo diet but before that i was mostly vegan ------ go for (Epi-Paleo-RX) + (CT)+ be diligent about maintaining good Circadian Rhythm

> i just started taking BHRT but don't want to take , long term.. would rather try to balance my hormones, naturally.
> i've been told that i have some liver blood stagnation issues which would explain possible methylation issues..

There are limits when attempting natural hormonal balance, that mostly apply to hormones that correlate with circadian rhythm (cortisol, melatonin), most other steroid hormones you should manage for life.

BHRT, describe what are you taking
\==============================================================
DHEAs(Women 275-400) μg/dL
pregnenolone(<151)ng/dL

Easiest way to make some dent in high cholesterol would be to take pills and incerase pregnenolone and DHEAs to values close to the top ranges as above.
Any DHEA pills would do, but be consistent with manufacturer pills that you use.
My self I have good experience with pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology.

That may also increase your low cortisol and help with progesterone management during cycle.

You have very high SHBG. SHBG binds testosterone and estrogens. Will be hard to lower high estrogens.
OTOH, estrogens may go lower if you work on their excretion rate.
Eat cruciferous vegetables, add som calcium-D-glutarate.
Eventually you may need to use Arimidex (prescription drug, aromatase inhibitor), but that latter.

\-------------------------------------
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio)

Consider getting this two tests done, sooner, better:
scan each page, post it.

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel
[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)
Price: $350.00

At labCorp or Mayo Clinic
Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum
insurance should pay for this test.

//////////////
check

homocysteine
B12
B6
Folic Acid

The last three are part of Spectracell, may skip in blood testing.
////////

# Thread: Seasonality and Food:  An attempt to deepen my understanding
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-food-an-attempt-to-deepen-my-understanding.9422/>

### Curves — 2014-01-21T19:54:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-food-an-attempt-to-deepen-my-understanding.9422/post-106485>

Hey Everyone! I've been pondering this question on seasonality and food and looking for some guidance. I know there is a piece missing in my understanding and would appreciate some help in figuring it out. Here goes:

There are two parallel Circadian systems.
One, the master "clock" located in the SCN (suprachiasmatic nucleus) is entrained to light. And not to any other environmental cue.
The other system exists in the peripheral tissues such as the gut and are (or can be) synced to food.
A mismatch occurs when these two are on different pages such as eating carbs in low light cycles.
Here's where I get derailed.
If blue light at night tells your body it's summer and you eat carbs does a mismatch occur?
I'm guessing yes, but wondering what's the missing piece in my understanding.

So nice to be back in this community

### Inger — 2014-01-21T20:34:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-food-an-attempt-to-deepen-my-understanding.9422/post-106490>

Nice to have you back

IDK but I just think we cannot fool nature... It is just not made to have the same season all year so we would suffer if we artificially make it like that.
and carbs need sunlight... not blue light. They need the real sun and vit D to be healthy for us. So artificial blue light would be no option to make carbs healthy as only the sun makes, when it is strong in summer.

this was very non geeky lol but hope it helps a little still!

PS. I personally think carbs are not even good in summer if we are not tanning a lot, like modern human, indoors all day and much clothing on. I bet carbs are not good in those conditions either

### Shijin13 — 2014-01-21T20:58:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-food-an-attempt-to-deepen-my-understanding.9422/post-106492>

Don't forget the SCN senses both Light and Magnetism. the mismatch of carbs and seasons = decreased energy efficiency, especially in low light cycles. If you're ill they can also be a bad thing as well if you're not getting outside to spend time to increase your energy potential through the photoelectric effect. as Jack said last night - everything is energy - from the photoelectric effect to magnetism - it all is energy that directly impacts how your body leverages that energy in water and food....

### Curves — 2014-01-21T21:03:48+0200

I'm guessing again the answer is yes but how exactly are humans "seasonal?"

I'm focused now on healing my gut with a lot of bone broth and probiotics and am curious how this will impact my ability to handle carbs in the future. Right now, I severely limit them all year round but I keep experimenting and testing my limits at the same time because nothing is static and health is always in motion.

# Thread: leptin reset on light cycle or waking?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-on-light-cycle-or-waking.9423/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### wonder — 2014-01-21T23:04:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-on-light-cycle-or-waking.9423/post-106507>

hi. i have a question: when doing the leptin reset as part of getting hormones and general system / metabolism in order, is it more important to follow light cycles in the morning or with our actual waking time? i ask because as part of a spiritual community, i generally wake up around 4 or 5am, hydrate with water and practice meditation and do energy work/cultivation for a few hours before eating. would it be possible to still wake up this early and not eat the BAB until 8 am since it is in line with the circadian rhythm for digestion, etc? i am aware from ct-7 that ghrelin has a half life etc. and that there are other hormonal firings taking place at waking and soon after...
on such a schedule i would eat dinner around 3 or 4 and sleep around 8 or 9pm with only a very light snack if anything for lunch.
i am not looking to lose weight and my sleep is actually quite good. i am mainly working at healing my system after over 5 years of undetected parasites that wreaked havoc on all organs and systems (they have been removed from body and i am on a prescribed herbal healing regimen. additionally looking to regain 20-30 lbs of lost weight and get a thyroid back into order, and boost general energy. body tends to be cold.

thanks so much for input.

### margor_j — 2014-01-21T23:25:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-on-light-cycle-or-waking.9423/post-106509>

hi there,
wow, sounds like youre in a nice space! something im going to entertain doing again... im relatively new to this forum, and i have seen that question asked more than once. my understand is: you eat within half an hour of waking. this is important to reset ancient pathways that were in use before leptin came along. some mornings (rarely now) if i wake at stupid o'clock (like 4am?!) then yeah, i struggle to eat that early to, dont wanna wake ppl up etc.
iv had some really good guidance on here, and some of the senior members have let me know (in a nice way!) to pull my finger out and do it to the book. so for me, that means eating within half an hour of waking. and if thats really early, then i look at the sun when i can.

hope that helps - im still learning a lot tho, like i said!

namaste
Mags

A tour using cross fit locations as their jumping off point. ### freesia — 2014-01-22T23:09:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-webinar.9427/post-106644>

I've been avoiding nuts because avoiding high arginine foods is supposed to (backed up by n=1) help with those experiencing reactivating EBV and other HHVs. Theres likely a bigger picture there I haven't yet got my head around. I do have macadamias and walnuts very occasionally though, and pine nuts (low arginine) regularly but I doubt I eat enough to support a robust bifidobacteria count.
Definitely need to give this some more thought yet and figure out how to best approach given my context.

A couple of interesting (to me anyway lol) things... 2 years implementing protocols here I can now tolerate nightshades...at least some overground ones... tomatoes capsicums and aubergine. No joint swelling at all after eating these this summer! (First summer since 1994!)
I'm game to give potato starch a fair trial personally. I have been making my own potato starch, but that's not sustainable if I want to take bigger doses. 3 medium potatoes makes around 1 Tbsp.
The other thing... I noticed a couple of people, not eating ketogenically, say they can now tolerate potatoes where they couldn't before due to salicylate sensitivity. Possibly they've built up bifidobacteria to where they can degrade salicylates effectively? I'm just guessing there.

Whole nuts would be my preference long term, over _part_ of a potato. Just my initial thoughts....

### Hope — 2014-01-23T01:05:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-webinar.9427/post-106650>

> [Paul S said:](/goto/post?id=106643)
>
> For those fixing a bad gut, or with an AI, on one hand it sounds possibly helpful to add resistant starches (avoiding nightshades) and IF'ing to improve gut health based on Jack's biohack. What about raw nuts and cocoa/seeds in those circumstances, however? Recommended, or dependent on each person's N=1 experience with those foods?

Hi Paul, I don't have (based on thyroid AB anyway) any thyroid AI issues. I do have a bad gut that I have been working on for years now. I do eat some nuts. I prefer the soaked kind, but I also do some roasted. I get them in little packets from one brand online and it is great for traveling. THough the actual nut is awesome too. I have tested my n-1 and seem to be fine in some ways, with some nuts. It is my only source of carbs outside veggies (no starchy ones unless you consider carrots starchy - I have not listened to the webinar) but I love them for the fat. I do use raw cocoa powder. I found the most amazing brand after trying a few. This one is totally mild. No bitter taste, no heavy metals, nothing. I gave some to my mom and she said she doesn't believe there is no sugar, but there isn't. I don't eat much of it - you don't need much, but I mix it with the nuts or nut butter as a treat...and wow. Amazing. I don't need ANY fruit, or starch....I do realize I gotta watch it, be careful of getting allergic.....or addicted.

I have eaten some white potatoes in summer and do not tolerate them. I can totally tell I get angry after I eat them. It is crazy I know but true. I have had sweet potatoes in the past but find I don't sleep as well when I eat them. I sleep WAY better without any carbs, besides the nuts (don't see anything bad there it appears). It is really amazing to me, and so worth it. And I thought maybe it was the magnetico and darkness. But no. It is more than that. Definitely fun to hack it.

And so fun to read everyone else's story. I do need to have a period again of not eating nuts at all. Just to see what happens. I am so thankful Dr K shared his results with us, just so cool!!! I wish I could do labs as often too. I really need to be a NP. LOL.

[http://www.wildernessfamilynaturals...oducts-raw-cacao-powder-fermented/CACAOP5.php](http://www.wildernessfamilynaturals.com/product/chocolate-products-raw-cacao-powder-fermented/CACAOP5.php)

The cacao powder is created by cold-pressing raw, organic cacao beans into a pressed cake separating the oil from the protein and fiber. The temperature is never allowed to exceed 120Â° F and is usually 104Â° F (all other cocoa powders are exposed to temperatures as high as 300Â° F!). A high-accuracy thermometer monitors the process. The remaining dry 'cake-like' material is then cold-ground and fine-milled so that only the finest raw cacao particles become the finished product.

This raw cacao powder has antioxidant activity as exhibited by an ORAC score of 1377 μmol TE/g.

### Josh — 2014-01-23T07:42:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-webinar.9427/post-106693>

Things to consider when gardening....

1\. Soil is built on the right substrates, organic and inorganic. I consider the fiber as a substrate to grow the bugs on.
2\. The fiber, wherever it comes from of whatever type is a substrate and in some cases a food for the microbiome.
3\. Fibers such as Arabinogalactan, typically sourced from Birch as I recall, support butyrate production if the right bugs are there. In my experience, this is the most useful fiber for most people with bad guts.
4\. Protein digestion, especially animal sources is shockingly dependent on manganese and molybdenum for many people. Almost overnight, I went from not being able to digest pork, eggs and any excess of other types and meats with high amounts of fat to be able to handle normal portions. I eat fat of various animal types by the spoon now. However, if I get more inflamed due to stress, a cold, whatever, I may lose the ability to digest these for a few days. I suspect that hormones may play a significant role for many people, especially women as well.
5\. If I have the right cofactors such as micronutrients and whatever else my liver may need, I seem to be able to shift my diet without disturbing my guts as evidenced by Bristol stool chart quality and regularity. This is a big if. When people are more inflamed and adrenal stressed, it seems that they handle a plant based diet with low fat protein such as fish in or chicken in moderate quantities better than the higher fat and protein paleo and epi-paleo prescriptions.
6\. Don't forget seasons and travel to other places with other electromagnetics and temperatures, etc...
7\. Parasites will throw the whole thing off quickly and most people have no clue as to whether they have them. They become active and a big deal when people get stressed from other things, you name it. I find they come out to play on the tail of acute respiratory infections in many people.

### freesia — 2014-01-23T23:50:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-webinar.9427/post-106831>

I have actually had a mouldy pumpkin seed - years ago. Horrible, and I didn't touch them again for ages! I'm sensitive to mould (though CT makes me stronger in the face of accidental exposures).
The ones I've been buying seem fine....no ill effects. I don't use them as a regular part of my diet, more intermittently.
